question_text: Find the solution of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=2^{y-x}{/tex}
question_html: Find the solution of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=2^{y-x}{/tex}
solution_text: Given that, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 2 y-x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2^{y}}{2^{x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{2^{y}}=\frac{d x}{2^{x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int 2^{-y} d y=\int 2^{-x} d x{/tex} (Integrating both sides) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-2^{-y}}{\log 2}=\frac{-2^{-x}}{\log 2}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2 -y + 2 -x = C log 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 -x - 2 -y = C log 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 -x - 2 -y = K [Where, K = C log 2]
solution_html:
Given that, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 2y-x 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2^{y}}{2^{x}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{2^{y}}=\frac{d x}{2^{x}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\int 2^{-y} d y=\int 2^{-x} d x{/tex} (Integrating both sides)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{-2^{-y}}{\log 2}=\frac{-2^{-x}}{\log 2}+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -2-y + 2-x = C log 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2-x - 2-y = C log 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2-x - 2-y = K [Where, K = C log 2]

question_text: Find the differential equation of all non-vertical lines in a plane.
question_html: Find the differential equation of all non-vertical lines in a plane.
solution_text: Since, the family of all non-vertical line is y = mx + c, where {tex}m \ne \tan \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} . On differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = m{/tex} again, differentiating w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
Since, the family of all non-vertical line is y = mx + c, where {tex}m \ne \tan \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}.
On differentiating w.r.t. x, we get

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = m{/tex}
again, differentiating w.r.t x, we get

{tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = 0{/tex}

question_text: Given that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = {e^{ - 2y}}{/tex} and y = 0 when x = 5. Find the value of x when y = 3.
question_html: Given that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = {e^{ - 2y}}{/tex} and y = 0 when x = 5. Find the value of x when y = 3.
solution_text: Given that, {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = {e^{ - 2y}} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{{e^{ - 2y}}}} = dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int {{e^{2y}}dy} = \int {dx \Rightarrow \frac{{{e^{2y}}}}{2}=x + c} {/tex} ..(i) When x = 5 and y = 0, then substituting these values in Eq. (i), we get {tex}\frac{{{e^0}}}{2} = 5 + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} = 5 + C \Rightarrow C = \frac{1}{2} - 5 = - \frac{9}{2}{/tex} Eq. (i) becomes e 2y = 2x - 9 when y = 3, then {tex}{e^6} = 2x - 9 \Rightarrow 2x = {e^6} + 9{/tex} {tex}\therefore x = \frac{{\left( {{e^6} + 9} \right)}}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that, {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = {e^{ - 2y}} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{{e^{ - 2y}}}} = dx{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \int {{e^{2y}}dy} = \int {dx \Rightarrow \frac{{{e^{2y}}}}{2}=x + c} {/tex} ..(i)

When x = 5 and y = 0, then substituting these values in Eq. (i), we get

{tex}\frac{{{e^0}}}{2} = 5 + C{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} = 5 + C \Rightarrow C = \frac{1}{2} - 5 = - \frac{9}{2}{/tex}

Eq. (i) becomes e2y = 2x - 9

when y = 3, then {tex}{e^6} = 2x - 9 \Rightarrow 2x = {e^6} + 9{/tex}

{tex}\therefore x = \frac{{\left( {{e^6} + 9} \right)}}{2}{/tex}

question_text: Solve the differential equation (x 2 - 1) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y=\frac{1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex}
question_html: Solve the differential equation (x2 - 1) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y=\frac{1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex}
solution_text: Given that, {tex}\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y=\frac{1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1}\right) y=\frac{1}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} which is a linear differential equation. On comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , we get P = {tex}\frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} , Q = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1} d x}=e^{\log \left(x^{2}-1\right)}{/tex} = x 2 - 1 Thus, solution is: {tex}y(\mathrm{I}.\mathrm{F})=\int Q(\mathrm{I.F}) d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x 2 - 1) = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}} \cdot\left(x^{2}-1\right) d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(x 2 - 1) = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}-1}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\left(x^{2}-1\right)=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right)+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that,
{tex}\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y=\frac{1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1}\right) y=\frac{1}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} 
which is a linear differential equation.
On comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex}, we get
P = {tex}\frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1}{/tex}, Q = {tex}\frac{1}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} 
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int \frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1} d x}=e^{\log \left(x^{2}-1\right)}{/tex} = x2 - 1
Thus, solution is: 
{tex}y(\mathrm{I}.\mathrm{F})=\int Q(\mathrm{I.F}) d x+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x2 - 1) = {tex}\int \frac{1}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}} \cdot\left(x^{2}-1\right) d x+C{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(x2 - 1) = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}-1}+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\left(x^{2}-1\right)=\frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right)+C{/tex}

question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y=y{/tex}
question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+2 x y=y{/tex}
solution_text: We have, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + 2xy = y {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=y(1-2 x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{y}{/tex} = (1 - 2x) dx {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y}=\int(1-2 x) d x{/tex} (Integrating both sides) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = x - x 2 + log C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y - log C = x - x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log {tex}\frac{y}{C}{/tex} = x - x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{C}{/tex} = {tex}x-x^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=C e^{x-x^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + 2xy = y
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=y(1-2 x){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{y}{/tex} = (1 - 2x) dx
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d y}{y}=\int(1-2 x) d x{/tex} (Integrating both sides)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y = x - x2 + log C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log y - log C = x - x2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log {tex}\frac{y}{C}{/tex} = x - x2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{C}{/tex} = {tex}x-x^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow y=C e^{x-x^{2}}{/tex}

question_text: Find the general solution of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+a y=e^{m x}{/tex}
question_html: Find the general solution of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+a y=e^{m x}{/tex}
solution_text: Given: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+a y=e^{m . x}{/tex} We need to find the solution of the given differential equation It is a first order linear differential equation. Comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p(x) y=q(x){/tex} p(x) = a q(x) = e x.m Calculating integrating factor I.F. = {tex}e^{\int p(x)} d x{/tex} I.F. = {tex}e^{\int a d x}{/tex} I.F. = e ax Therefore, the solution of the differential equation is given by y.(I.F.) = {tex}\int \mathrm{q}(\mathrm{x}) \cdot(\mathrm{IF}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} y.(e ax ) = {tex}\int e^{\operatorname{mx}} e^{a x} d x{/tex} y.(e ax ) = {tex}\int e^{(m+a) x} d x{/tex} Formula: {tex}\int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{ax}} \mathrm{dx}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{ax}}{/tex} {tex}y \cdot\left(e^{a x}\right)=\frac{\left(e^{(m+a) x}\right)}{m+a}+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+a y=e^{m . x}{/tex} 
We need to find the solution of the given differential equation
It is a first order linear differential equation.
Comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+p(x) y=q(x){/tex} 
p(x) = a
q(x) = ex.m 
Calculating integrating factor
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int p(x)} d x{/tex} 
I.F. = {tex}e^{\int a d x}{/tex} 
I.F. = eax 
Therefore, the solution of the differential equation is given by
y.(I.F.) = {tex}\int \mathrm{q}(\mathrm{x}) \cdot(\mathrm{IF}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} 
y.(eax) = {tex}\int e^{\operatorname{mx}} e^{a x} d x{/tex} 
y.(eax) = {tex}\int e^{(m+a) x} d x{/tex} 
Formula: {tex}\int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{ax}} \mathrm{dx}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{ax}}{/tex} 
{tex}y \cdot\left(e^{a x}\right)=\frac{\left(e^{(m+a) x}\right)}{m+a}+C{/tex}

question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+1=e^{x+y}{/tex}
question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+1=e^{x+y}{/tex}
solution_text: Given: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+1=e^{x+y}{/tex} To find solution of the given differential equation Assuming x + y = t Differentiating both sides with respect to x {tex}1+\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} Substituting {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+1=e^{x+y}{/tex} in the above equation. {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}}=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} e t = {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} Re-writing the equation as dx = e -t dt Integrating both sides {tex}\int \mathrm{dx}=\int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}{/tex} Formula: {tex}\int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} x = -e -t + C Substituting t = x + y x = -e -t + C is the solution of the given differential equation.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+1=e^{x+y}{/tex} 
To find solution of the given differential equation
Assuming x + y = t
Differentiating both sides with respect to x
{tex}1+\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} 
Substituting {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+1=e^{x+y}{/tex} in the above equation.
{tex}\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}}=\frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} 
et = {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}
Re-writing the equation as
dx = e-t dt
Integrating both sides
{tex}\int \mathrm{dx}=\int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}{/tex} 
Formula: {tex}\int \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} 
x = -e-t + C
Substituting t = x + y
x = -e-t + C  is the solution of the given differential equation.

question_text: Solve: ydx - xdy = x 2 ydx.
question_html: Solve: ydx - xdy = x2ydx.
solution_text: ATQ, given equation is : ydx - xdy = x 2 dx Hence , We need to find the solution of the given differential equation. Re-writing the given equation as {tex}y-x \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{2} y{/tex} {tex}y-x \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{2} y{/tex} {tex}\frac{1-x^{2}}{x} d x=\frac{d y}{y}{/tex} Integrating both sides {tex}\int \frac{1-x^{2}}{x} d x=\int \frac{d y}{y}{/tex} {tex}\int \frac{1}{x}-x d x=\int \frac{d y}{y}{/tex} Formula: {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} = In x {tex}\int x^{n} d x=\frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}{/tex} In x - {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + In c = In y {tex}-\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} = In y - In x - In c {tex}-\frac{x^{2}}{2}=\ln \frac{y}{c x}{/tex} y = {tex}c e^{-\frac{x^{2}}{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
ATQ, given equation is :  ydx - xdy = x2dx
Hence , We need to find the  solution of the given differential equation.
Re-writing the given equation as
{tex}y-x \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{2} y{/tex} 
{tex}y-x \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{2} y{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{1-x^{2}}{x} d x=\frac{d y}{y}{/tex} 
Integrating both sides 
{tex}\int \frac{1-x^{2}}{x} d x=\int \frac{d y}{y}{/tex} 
{tex}\int \frac{1}{x}-x d x=\int \frac{d y}{y}{/tex} 
Formula: {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} = In x
{tex}\int x^{n} d x=\frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}{/tex} 
In x - {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} + In c = In y
{tex}-\frac{x^{2}}{2}{/tex} = In y - In x - In c
{tex}-\frac{x^{2}}{2}=\ln \frac{y}{c x}{/tex} 
y = {tex}c e^{-\frac{x^{2}}{2}}{/tex}

question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 1 + x + {y^2} + x{y^2}{/tex} , when y = 0, x = 0.
question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 1 + x + {y^2} + x{y^2}{/tex}, when y = 0, x = 0.
solution_text: Given that, {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 1 + x + {y^2} + x{y^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \left( {1 + x} \right) + {y^2}\left( {1 + x} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \left( {1 + {y^2}} \right)\left( {1 + x} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{1 + {y^2}}} = \left( {1 + x} \right)dx{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}y = x + \frac{{{x^2}}}{2} + K{/tex} ...(i) When y=0 and x=0. then substituting these values in Eq. (i), we get {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( 0 \right) = 0 + 0 + K{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow K = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\tan ^{ - 1}}y = x + \frac{{{x^2}}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = \tan \left( {x + \frac{{{x^2}}}{2}} \right){/tex}
solution_html:
Given that, {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 1 + x + {y^2} + x{y^2}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \left( {1 + x} \right) + {y^2}\left( {1 + x} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \left( {1 + {y^2}} \right)\left( {1 + x} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{1 + {y^2}}} = \left( {1 + x} \right)dx{/tex}

On integrating both sides, we get

{tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}y = x + \frac{{{x^2}}}{2} + K{/tex} ...(i)

When y=0 and x=0. then substituting these values in Eq. (i), we get

{tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( 0 \right) = 0 + 0 + K{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow K = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {\tan ^{ - 1}}y = x + \frac{{{x^2}}}{2}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y = \tan \left( {x + \frac{{{x^2}}}{2}} \right){/tex}

question_text: Find the general solution of (x + 2y 3 ) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y
question_html: Find the general solution of (x + 2y3) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y
solution_text: Given: {tex}\left(x+2 y^{3}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=y{/tex} Re-writing the equation as {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{\left(x+2 y^{3}\right)}{y}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{x}{y}+2 y^{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{x}{y}=2 y^{2}{/tex} It is a first order linear differential equation. Comparing it with {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+p(y) x=q(y){/tex} p(y) = - {tex}\frac{1}{y}{/tex} q(y) = 2y 2 Calculating integrating factor I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{{\int} \mathrm{p}(\mathrm{y}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{/tex} I.F. = {tex}e^{-\int \frac{1}{y} d y}{/tex} I.F. = e -In y I.F. = {tex}\frac 1y{/tex} Therefore, the solution of the differential equation is given by x.(I.F.) = {tex}\int \mathrm{q}(\mathrm{y}) \cdot(\mathrm{IF}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{y}=\int 2 y d y{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}{/tex} = y 2 + C x = y 3 + Cy
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\left(x+2 y^{3}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=y{/tex} 
Re-writing the equation as
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{\left(x+2 y^{3}\right)}{y}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{x}{y}+2 y^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{x}{y}=2 y^{2}{/tex} 
It is a first order linear differential equation.
Comparing it with {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}+p(y) x=q(y){/tex} 
p(y) = -{tex}\frac{1}{y}{/tex}
q(y) = 2y2 
Calculating integrating factor
I.F. = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{{\int} \mathrm{p}(\mathrm{y}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{/tex} 
I.F. = {tex}e^{-\int \frac{1}{y} d y}{/tex} 
I.F. = e-In y 
I.F. = {tex}\frac 1y{/tex}
Therefore, the solution of the differential equation is given by
x.(I.F.) = {tex}\int \mathrm{q}(\mathrm{y}) \cdot(\mathrm{IF}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{x}{y}=\int 2 y d y{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}{/tex} = y2 + C 
x = y3 + Cy

question_text: If y(x) is a solution of {tex}\left( {\frac{{2 + \sin x}}{{1 + y}}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \cos x{/tex} and y(0) = 1 then find the value of {tex}y\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex}
question_html: If y(x) is a solution of {tex}\left( {\frac{{2 + \sin x}}{{1 + y}}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \cos x{/tex} and y(0) = 1 then find the value of {tex}y\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex}
solution_text: Given that, {tex}\left( {\frac{{2 + \sin x}}{{1 + y}}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \cos x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{1 + y}} = - \frac{{\cos x}}{{2 + \ sinx }}dx{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int \frac{1}{1+y}dy= - \int \frac{cos x}{2 + \sin x}dx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left( {1 + y} \right) = - \log \left( {2 + \sin x} \right) + \log C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left( {1 + y} \right) + \log \left( {2 + \sin x} \right) = \log C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log \left( {1 + y} \right)\left( {2 + \sin x} \right) = \log C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 + y} \right)\left( {2 + \sin x} \right) = C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1 + y = \frac{C}{{2 + \sin x}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac{C}{{2 + \sin x}} - 1{/tex} ..(i) When x = 0, y = 1 then {tex}1 = \frac{C}{2} - 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow C = 4{/tex} On putting C = 4 in Eq. (i), we get {tex}y = \frac{4}{{2 + \sin x}} - 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore y\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right) = \frac{4}{{2 + \sin \frac{\pi }{2}}} - 1 = \frac{4}{{2 + 1}} - 1{/tex} {tex}= \frac{4}{3} - 1 = \frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that, {tex}\left( {\frac{{2 + \sin x}}{{1 + y}}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \cos x{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{1 + y}} = - \frac{{\cos x}}{{2 + \ sinx }}dx{/tex}

On integrating both sides, we get

{tex}\int \frac{1}{1+y}dy= - \int \frac{cos x}{2 + \sin x}dx{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left( {1 + y} \right) = - \log \left( {2 + \sin x} \right) + \log C{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left( {1 + y} \right) + \log \left( {2 + \sin x} \right) = \log C{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \log \left( {1 + y} \right)\left( {2 + \sin x} \right) = \log C{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 + y} \right)\left( {2 + \sin x} \right) = C{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 1 + y = \frac{C}{{2 + \sin x}}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac{C}{{2 + \sin x}} - 1{/tex} ..(i)

When x = 0, y = 1 then

{tex}1 = \frac{C}{2} - 1{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow C = 4{/tex}

On putting C = 4 in Eq. (i), we get

{tex}y = \frac{4}{{2 + \sin x}} - 1{/tex}

{tex}\therefore y\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right) = \frac{4}{{2 + \sin \frac{\pi }{2}}} - 1 = \frac{4}{{2 + 1}} - 1{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{4}{3} - 1 = \frac{1}{3}{/tex}

question_text: If y(t) is a solution of (1 + t) {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}{/tex} - ty = 1 and y (0) = - 1, then show that y (1) = - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
question_html: If y(t) is a solution of (1 + t) {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}{/tex} - ty = 1 and y (0) = - 1, then show that y (1) = -{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution_text: Given: {tex}(1+t) \frac{d y}{d t}-t y{/tex} = 1 (0, -1) is a solution. Re-writing the given equation as {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}-\frac{t y}{1+t}=\frac{1}{1+t}{/tex} It is a first order linear differential equation Comparing it with {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} + p(t)y = q(t) p(t) = {tex}-\frac{t}{1+t}{/tex} q(t) = {tex}\frac{1}{1+t}{/tex} Calculating integrating factor IF = {tex}e^{-\int \frac{t}{1+t} d t}{/tex} IF = {tex}e^{\int \frac{-t+1-1}{1+t} d t}{/tex} IF = {tex}e^{\int-1+\frac{1}{1+t} d t}{/tex} IF = {tex}e^{-t+\ln (1+t)}{/tex} IF = e -t (1 + t) Therefore, the solution of the differential equation is given by y.(IF) = {tex}\int \mathrm{q}(\mathrm{t}) \cdot(\mathrm{IF}) \mathrm{dt}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{y}\left(\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t}}(1+\mathrm{t})\right)=\int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t}}(1+\mathrm{t}) \frac{1}{1+\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}{/tex} y(e -t (1 + t)) = {tex}\int e^{-t} d t{/tex} y(e -t (1 + t)) = -e -t + C Substituting (0, -1) to find the value of C. -1 = -1 + C C = 0 y(e -t (1 + t)) = -e -t is a solution therefore y(1) is y(1) = {tex}-\frac{1}{1+1}=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}(1+t) \frac{d y}{d t}-t y{/tex} = 1 (0, -1) is a solution.
Re-writing the given equation as
{tex}\frac{d y}{d t}-\frac{t y}{1+t}=\frac{1}{1+t}{/tex} 
It is a first order linear differential equation
Comparing it with {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} + p(t)y = q(t)
p(t) = {tex}-\frac{t}{1+t}{/tex} 
q(t) = {tex}\frac{1}{1+t}{/tex} 
Calculating integrating factor
IF = {tex}e^{-\int \frac{t}{1+t} d t}{/tex} 
IF = {tex}e^{\int \frac{-t+1-1}{1+t} d t}{/tex} 
IF = {tex}e^{\int-1+\frac{1}{1+t} d t}{/tex} 
IF = {tex}e^{-t+\ln (1+t)}{/tex} 
IF = e-t(1 + t)
Therefore, the solution of the differential equation is given by
y.(IF) = {tex}\int \mathrm{q}(\mathrm{t}) \cdot(\mathrm{IF}) \mathrm{dt}{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{y}\left(\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t}}(1+\mathrm{t})\right)=\int \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t}}(1+\mathrm{t}) \frac{1}{1+\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{dt}{/tex} 
y(e-t(1 + t)) = {tex}\int e^{-t} d t{/tex} 
y(e-t(1 + t)) = -e-t + C
Substituting (0, -1) to find the value of C.
-1 = -1 + C
C = 0
y(e-t(1 + t)) = -e-t is a solution
therefore y(1) is
y(1) = {tex}-\frac{1}{1+1}=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

question_text: Form the differential equation having y = (sin -1 x) 2 + Acos -1 x + B where A and B are arbitrary constants, as its general solution.
question_html: Form the differential equation having y = (sin-1x)2 + Acos-1x + B where A and B are arbitrary constants, as its general solution.
solution_text: Given that, y = (sin -1 x) 2 + Acos -1 x + B On differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{2{{\sin }^{ - 1}}x}}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} + \frac{{\left( { - A} \right)}}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2{\sin ^{ - 1}}x - A{/tex} Again, differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} + \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}.\frac{{ - 2x}}{{2\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} = \frac{2}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 - {x^2}} \right)\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - \frac{x}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}.\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 - {x^2}} \right)\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 - {x^2}} \right)\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - 2 = 0{/tex} Which is the required differential equation.
solution_html:
Given that, y = (sin-1x)2 + Acos-1x + B

On differentiating w.r.t. x, we get

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{2{{\sin }^{ - 1}}x}}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} + \frac{{\left( { - A} \right)}}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2{\sin ^{ - 1}}x - A{/tex}

Again, differentiating w.r.t. x, we get

{tex}\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} + \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}.\frac{{ - 2x}}{{2\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} = \frac{2}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 - {x^2}} \right)\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - \frac{x}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}.\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 - {x^2}} \right)\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 - {x^2}} \right)\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - 2 = 0{/tex}

Which is the required differential equation.

question_text: Form the differential equation of all circles which pass through origin and whose centers lie on y-axis.
question_html: Form the differential equation of all circles which pass through origin and whose centers lie on y-axis.
solution_text: It is given that, circles pass through origin and their centres lie on Y-axis. Let (0, k) be the centre of the circle and radius is k. So, the equation of circle is {tex}{\left( {x - 0} \right)^2} + {\left( {y - k} \right)^2} = {k^2}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} + \left( {y^2 +{k^2}-2ky} \right) = {k^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {x^2} + {y^2} - 2ky = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{x^2} + {y^2}}}{{2y}} = k{/tex} ...(i) On differentiating Eq. (i) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{2y\left( {2x + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right) - \left( {{x^2} +{y^2}} \right)\frac{{2dy}}{{dx}}}}{{4{y^2}}} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 4y\left( {x + y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right) - 2\left( {{x^2} + {y^2}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 4xy + 4{y^2}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - 2\left( {{x^2} + {y^2}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {4{y^2} - 2\left( {{x^2} + {y^2}} \right)} \right]\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + 4xy = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {4{y^2} - 2{x^2} - 2{y^2}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + 4xy = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {2{y^2} - 2{x^2}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + 4xy = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {{y^2} - {x^2}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + 2xy = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {{x^2} - {y^2}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - 2xy = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that, circles pass through origin and their centres lie on Y-axis. Let (0, k) be the centre of the circle and radius is k.

So, the equation of circle is

{tex}{\left( {x - 0} \right)^2} + {\left( {y - k} \right)^2} = {k^2}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} + \left( {y^2 +{k^2}-2ky} \right) = {k^2}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow {x^2} + {y^2} - 2ky = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{x^2} + {y^2}}}{{2y}} = k{/tex} ...(i)

On differentiating Eq. (i) w.r.t. x, we get

{tex}\frac{{2y\left( {2x + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right) - \left( {{x^2} +{y^2}} \right)\frac{{2dy}}{{dx}}}}{{4{y^2}}} = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow 4y\left( {x + y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right) - 2\left( {{x^2} + {y^2}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 4xy + 4{y^2}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - 2\left( {{x^2} + {y^2}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ {4{y^2} - 2\left( {{x^2} + {y^2}} \right)} \right]\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + 4xy = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {4{y^2} - 2{x^2} - 2{y^2}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + 4xy = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {2{y^2} - 2{x^2}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + 4xy = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {{y^2} - {x^2}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + 2xy = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {{x^2} - {y^2}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - 2xy = 0{/tex}

question_text: Find the equation of a curve passing through origin and satisfying the differential equation {tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + 2xy = 4{x^2}{/tex}
question_html: Find the equation of a curve passing through origin and satisfying the differential equation {tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + 2xy = 4{x^2}{/tex}
solution_text: Given that, {tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + 2xy = 4{x^2}{/tex} Dividing both sides by {tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \frac{{2x}}{{1 + {x^2}}}.y = \frac{{4{x^2}}}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} which is a linear differential equation. On comparing it with {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + Py = Q,{/tex} we get {tex}P = \frac{{2x}}{{1 + {x^2}}},Q = \frac{{4{x^2}}}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore I.F = {e^{\int {Pdx} }} = {e^{\int {\frac{{2x}}{{1 + {x^2}}}} }}dx{/tex} Put {tex}1 + {x^2} = t \Rightarrow 2xdx = dt{/tex} {tex}I.F = 1 + {x^2} = {e^{\int {\frac{{dt}}{t}} }} = {e^{\log t}} = {e^{\log \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}=1+x^2{/tex} The general solution is {tex}y.\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right) = \int {\frac{{4{x^2}}}{{1 + {x^2}}}} \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)dx + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y.\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right) = \int {4{x^2}dx} + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y.\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right) = 4\frac{{{x^3}}}{3} + C{/tex} ...(i) Since, the curve passes through origin, then substituting x = 0 and y = 0 in Eq. (i), we get C = 0 The required equation of curve is {tex}y\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right) = \frac{{4{x^3}}}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac{{4{x^3}}}{{3\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that, {tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + 2xy = 4{x^2}{/tex}

Dividing both sides by {tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \frac{{2x}}{{1 + {x^2}}}.y = \frac{{4{x^2}}}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex}

which is a linear differential equation.

On comparing it with {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + Py = Q,{/tex} we get

{tex}P = \frac{{2x}}{{1 + {x^2}}},Q = \frac{{4{x^2}}}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore I.F = {e^{\int {Pdx} }} = {e^{\int {\frac{{2x}}{{1 + {x^2}}}} }}dx{/tex}

Put {tex}1 + {x^2} = t \Rightarrow 2xdx = dt{/tex}

{tex}I.F = 1 + {x^2} = {e^{\int {\frac{{dt}}{t}} }} = {e^{\log t}} = {e^{\log \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)}}{/tex}{tex}=1+x^2{/tex}

The general solution is

{tex}y.\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right) = \int {\frac{{4{x^2}}}{{1 + {x^2}}}} \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)dx + C{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y.\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right) = \int {4{x^2}dx} + C{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow y.\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right) = 4\frac{{{x^3}}}{3} + C{/tex} ...(i)

Since, the curve passes through origin, then substituting

x = 0 and y = 0 in Eq. (i), we get 

C = 0

The required equation of curve is

{tex}y\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right) = \frac{{4{x^3}}}{3}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac{{4{x^3}}}{{3\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)}}{/tex}

question_text: Solve: x 2 {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = x 2 + xy + y 2 .
question_html: Solve: x2{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = x2 + xy + y2.
solution_text: Given: {tex}\mathrm{x}^{2} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = x 2 + y 2 + xy To find : solution of the given differential equation Re-writing the given equation as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=1+\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+\frac{y}{x}{/tex} It is clearly a homogenous differential equation Assuming y = vx Differentiating both sides {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} Substituting {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} from the given equation {tex}1+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} {tex}1+\mathrm{v}^{2}+\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} 1 + v 2 = {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{x}=\frac{d v}{1+v^{2}}{/tex} Now integrating both sides {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}=\int \frac{d v}{1+v^{2}}{/tex} In x = tan v + C Formula: {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}=\ln x \int \frac{d v}{1+v^{2}}=\tan ^{-1} v{/tex} Substituting v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} In x = {tex}\tan ^{-1} \frac{y}{x}+C{/tex} is the solution of the given differential equation
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\mathrm{x}^{2} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = x2 + y2 + xy
To find: solution of the given differential equation
Re-writing the given equation as
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=1+\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}+\frac{y}{x}{/tex} 
It is clearly a homogenous differential equation
Assuming y = vx
Differentiating both sides
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} 
Substituting {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} from the given equation
{tex}1+\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{x}}=\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} 
{tex}1+\mathrm{v}^{2}+\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} 
1 + v2 = {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d x}{x}=\frac{d v}{1+v^{2}}{/tex} 
Now integrating both sides
{tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}=\int \frac{d v}{1+v^{2}}{/tex} 
In x = tan v + C
Formula: {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}=\ln x \int \frac{d v}{1+v^{2}}=\tan ^{-1} v{/tex} 
Substituting v = {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} 
In x = {tex}\tan ^{-1} \frac{y}{x}+C{/tex} is the solution of the given differential equation

question_text: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex} \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) + \left( x - e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex} .
question_html: Find the general solution of the differential equation {tex} \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) + \left( x - e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex} .
solution_text: According to the question, Given differential equation is {tex}\left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) + \left( x - e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex} It can be rewritten as {tex}\left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d x } { d y } + x - e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } = 0{/tex} On dividing both sides by {tex}(1 + y^2){/tex} , we get {tex}\frac { d x } { d y } + \frac { 1 } { \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) } x = \frac { e ^ { \tan } y ^ { - y } } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} It is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d x } { d y } + P x = Q{/tex} , here, {tex}P = \frac { 1 } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} and {tex}Q = \frac { e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} We know that, Integrating factor {tex}\mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { P } \mathrm { dy } }{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { \int \frac { 1 } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } } = e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The general solution of linear differential equation is given by {tex}x \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d y + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \times e ^ { { tan } ^ { - 1 } y } = \int \frac { e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } \times e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } d y + C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x{e^{ta{n^{ - 1}}y}} = \int {\frac{{{e^{2{{\tan }^{ - 1}}y}}}}{{1 + {y^2}}}} dy + C{/tex} put {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } y = t{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } d y = d t{/tex} {tex}x e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } = \int e ^ { 2 t } d t + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } = \frac { e ^ { 2 t } } { 2 } + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } = \frac { e ^ { 2 \tan ^ { - 1 } y } } { 2 } + C{/tex} {tex}[ \because t = tan^{-1 }y]{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,
Given differential equation is
{tex}\left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) + \left( x - e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex}
It can be rewritten as
{tex}\left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d x } { d y } + x - e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } = 0{/tex}
On dividing both sides by {tex}(1 + y^2){/tex}, we get
{tex}\frac { d x } { d y } + \frac { 1 } { \left( 1 + y ^ { 2 } \right) } x = \frac { e ^ { \tan } y ^ { - y } } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
It is a linear differential equation of the form {tex}\frac { d x } { d y } + P x = Q{/tex},
here, {tex}P = \frac { 1 } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex} and {tex}Q = \frac { e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
We know that,
Integrating factor {tex}\mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int \mathrm { P } \mathrm { dy } }{/tex}
{tex}= e ^ { \int \frac { 1 } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } } = e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The general solution of linear differential equation is given by {tex}x \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \mathrm { IF } ) d y + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \times e ^ { { tan } ^ { - 1 } y } = \int \frac { e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } \times e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } d y + C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x{e^{ta{n^{ - 1}}y}} = \int {\frac{{{e^{2{{\tan }^{ - 1}}y}}}}{{1 + {y^2}}}} dy + C{/tex}
put {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } y = t{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 1 + y ^ { 2 } } d y = d t{/tex}
{tex}x e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } = \int e ^ { 2 t } d t + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } = \frac { e ^ { 2 t } } { 2 } + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x e ^ { \tan ^ { - 1 } y } = \frac { e ^ { 2 \tan ^ { - 1 } y } } { 2 } + C{/tex} {tex}[ \because t = tan^{-1 }y]{/tex}

question_text: Find the general solution of y 2 dx + (x 2 - xy + y 2 )dy = 0.
question_html: Find the general solution of y2dx + (x2 - xy + y2)dy = 0.
solution_text: Given, differential equation is y 2 dx + (x 2 - xy + y 2 )dy = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow {y^2}dx = - \left( {{x^2} - xy + {y^2}} \right)dy{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {y^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = - \left( {{x^2} - xy + {y^2}} \right){/tex} Dividing both sides by y 2 ,we get {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = - \left( {\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{y^2}}} - \frac{x}{y} + 1} \right){/tex} ...(i) Which is a homogeneous differential equation. Put {tex}\frac{x}{y} = v{/tex} or x = vy {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = v + y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}}{/tex} On substituting these values in Eq. (i), we get {tex}v + y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = - \left[ {{v^2} - v + 1} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = - {v^2} + v - 1 - v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = - {v^2} - 1 \Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{{v^2} + 1}} = - \frac{{dy}}{y}{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int {\frac{{dv}}{{{v^2} + 1}}} = \int { - \frac{{dy}}{y}} {/tex} tan -1 (v) = -log y + C {tex}\Rightarrow {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{x}{y}} \right) + \log y = C\,\left[ {\because v = \frac{x}{y}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, differential equation is

y2dx + (x2 - xy + y2)dy = 0

{tex} \Rightarrow {y^2}dx = - \left( {{x^2} - xy + {y^2}} \right)dy{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow {y^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = - \left( {{x^2} - xy + {y^2}} \right){/tex}

Dividing both sides by y2,we get

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = - \left( {\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{y^2}}} - \frac{x}{y} + 1} \right){/tex} ...(i)

Which is a homogeneous differential equation.

Put {tex}\frac{x}{y} = v{/tex} or x = vy

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = v + y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}}{/tex}

On substituting these values in Eq. (i), we get

{tex}v + y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = - \left[ {{v^2} - v + 1} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = - {v^2} + v - 1 - v{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{{dv}}{{dy}} = - {v^2} - 1 \Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{{v^2} + 1}} = - \frac{{dy}}{y}{/tex}

On integrating both sides, we get

{tex}\int {\frac{{dv}}{{{v^2} + 1}}} = \int { - \frac{{dy}}{y}} {/tex}

tan-1(v) = -log y + C

{tex}\Rightarrow {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{x}{y}} \right) + \log y = C\,\left[ {\because v = \frac{x}{y}} \right]{/tex}

question_text: Solve: (x + y) (dx - dy) = dx + dy. [Hint: Substitute x + y = z after separting dx and dy]
question_html: Solve: (x + y) (dx - dy) = dx + dy. [Hint: Substitute x + y = z after separting dx and dy]
solution_text: Given: (x + y) (dx - dy) = dx + dy Re-writing the given equation as {tex}(x+y)\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} Assume x + y = z Differentiating both sides wrt to x {tex}1+\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d z}{d x}{/tex} Substituting this value in the given equation {tex}\mathrm{z}\left(1-\frac{\mathrm{dz}}{\mathrm{dx}}+1\right)=\frac{\mathrm{dz}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} {tex}2 z-z \frac{d z}{d x}-\frac{d z}{d x}={/tex} 0 2z = (z + 1) {tex}\frac{d z}{d x}{/tex} dx = {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2 z}\right) d z{/tex} Now integrating both sides {tex}\int \mathrm{dx}=\int\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{z}}\right) \mathrm{d} z{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \ln \mathrm{z}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} Substituting z = x + y x = {tex}\frac{x+y}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \ln (x+y)+\ln c{/tex} x - y - ln(x + y) - c = 0 ln(x + y) + ln c = x - y ln c(x + y) = x - y c (x + y) = e x-y x + y = {tex}\frac{1}{c}{/tex} (e x-y ) x + y = de x-y where d = {tex}\frac{1}{c}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: (x + y) (dx - dy) = dx + dy
Re-writing the given equation as
{tex}(x+y)\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} 
Assume x + y = z
Differentiating both sides wrt to x
{tex}1+\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d z}{d x}{/tex} 
Substituting this value in the given equation
{tex}\mathrm{z}\left(1-\frac{\mathrm{dz}}{\mathrm{dx}}+1\right)=\frac{\mathrm{dz}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} 
{tex}2 z-z \frac{d z}{d x}-\frac{d z}{d x}={/tex} 0
2z = (z + 1) {tex}\frac{d z}{d x}{/tex} 
dx = {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2 z}\right) d z{/tex} 
Now integrating both sides
{tex}\int \mathrm{dx}=\int\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{z}}\right) \mathrm{d} z{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{z}}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \ln \mathrm{z}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
Substituting z = x + y
x = {tex}\frac{x+y}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \ln (x+y)+\ln c{/tex} 
x - y - ln(x + y) - c = 0
ln(x + y) + ln c = x - y
ln c(x + y) = x - y
c (x + y) = ex-y
x + y = {tex}\frac{1}{c}{/tex} (ex-y)
x + y = dex-y
where d = {tex}\frac{1}{c}{/tex}

question_text: Solve: 2(y + 3) - xy {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 0, given that y(1) = - 2
question_html: Solve: 2(y + 3) - xy{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 0, given that y(1) = - 2
solution_text: Given: 2(y + 3) - xy {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 0 and (1, -2) is a solution. To find: solution of given differential equation. Re-writing the equation as {tex}2 \frac{d x}{x}=\frac{y d y}{y+3}{/tex} {tex}2 \frac{d x}{x}=\frac{[(y+3)-3] d y}{y+3}{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 d x}{x}=d y-\frac{3 d y}{y+3}{/tex} Integrating both sides {tex}\int \frac{2 \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}=\int \mathrm{dy}-\int \frac{3 \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}+3}{/tex} 2 ln x = y - 3 In (y + 3) + C Formula: {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} = In x Substituting (-2,1) to find the value of C 0 = -2 + C C = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 ln x = y - 3 ln (y + 3) + 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 ln x + 3 ln (y + 3) = y + 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 ln x + 3 ln (y + 3) = y + 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ln x 2 + ln (y + 3) 3 = y + 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 (y + 3) 3 = y + C
solution_html:
Given: 2(y + 3) - xy{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 0 and (1, -2) is a solution.
To find: solution of given differential equation.
Re-writing the equation as
{tex}2 \frac{d x}{x}=\frac{y d y}{y+3}{/tex} 
{tex}2 \frac{d x}{x}=\frac{[(y+3)-3] d y}{y+3}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{2 d x}{x}=d y-\frac{3 d y}{y+3}{/tex} 
Integrating both sides
{tex}\int \frac{2 \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}=\int \mathrm{dy}-\int \frac{3 \mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{y}+3}{/tex} 
2 ln x = y - 3 In (y + 3) + C
Formula: {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x}{/tex} = In x
Substituting (-2,1) to find the value of C
0 = -2 + C
C = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 ln x = y - 3 ln (y + 3) + 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 ln x + 3 ln (y + 3) = y + 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 ln x + 3 ln (y + 3) = y + 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ln x2 + ln (y + 3)3 = y + 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2(y + 3)3 = y + C

question_text: Solve the differential equation dy = cos x (2 - y cosec x) dx given that y = 2 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
question_html: Solve the differential equation dy = cos x (2 - y cosec x) dx given that y = 2 when x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
solution_text: Given: dy = cos x (2 - y cosec x)dx, where {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, 2\right){/tex} is a solution of the given equation. Re-writing the given equation as {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} = 2 cos x - y cot x {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} + y cot x = 2 cos x It is a first order linear differential equation Comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + p(x)y = q(x) p(x) = cot x q(x)=2 cos x Calculating integrating factor IF = {tex}e^{\int p(x)} d x{/tex} IF = {tex}e^{\int \cot x d x}{/tex} IF = {tex}e^{\ln \sin x}{/tex} IF = sin x Therefore, the solution of the differential equation is given by y.(IF) = {tex}\int \mathrm{q}(\mathrm{x}) \cdot(\mathrm{IF}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} y sin x = {tex}2 \int \cos x \sin x d x{/tex} y sin x = {tex}\int \sin 2 x d x{/tex} y sin x = - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} cos 2x + C Subsituting {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, 2\right){/tex} to get the value of C 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} + C C = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} Therefore, the solution is y sin x = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \cos 2 x+\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: dy = cos x (2 - y cosec x)dx, where {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, 2\right){/tex} is a solution of the given equation.
Re-writing the given equation as
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} = 2 cos x - y cot x
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} + y cot x = 2 cos x
It is a first order linear differential equation
Comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + p(x)y = q(x)
p(x) = cot x
q(x)=2 cos x
Calculating integrating factor
IF = {tex}e^{\int p(x)} d x{/tex}
IF = {tex}e^{\int \cot x d x}{/tex} 
IF = {tex}e^{\ln \sin x}{/tex} 
IF = sin x
Therefore, the solution of the differential equation is given by
y.(IF) = {tex}\int \mathrm{q}(\mathrm{x}) \cdot(\mathrm{IF}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} 
y sin x = {tex}2 \int \cos x \sin x d x{/tex} 
y sin x = {tex}\int \sin 2 x d x{/tex} 
y sin x = -{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}cos 2x + C
Subsituting {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}, 2\right){/tex} to get the value of C
2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} + C
C = {tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
Therefore, the solution is
y sin x = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \cos 2 x+\frac{3}{2}{/tex}

question_text: Form the differential equation by eliminating A and B in Ax 2 + By 2 = 1.
question_html: Form the differential equation by eliminating A and B in Ax2 + By2 = 1.
solution_text: Given equation is Ax 2 + By 2 = 1 On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}2Ax + 2By\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2By\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - 2Ax{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow By\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - Ax \Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{A}{B}{/tex} Again, differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{y}{x}.\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} + \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}.\left( {\frac{{x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - y}}{{{x^2}}}} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}.\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} + \frac{{x{{\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)}^2} -y \left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)}}{{{x^2}}} =0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow xy\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} + x{\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)^2} - y\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow xyy'' + x{\left( {y'} \right)^2} - yy' = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
Given equation is Ax2 + By2 = 1

On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get

{tex}2Ax + 2By\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow 2By\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - 2Ax{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow By\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - Ax \Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{A}{B}{/tex}

Again, differentiating w.r.t. x, we get

{tex}\frac{y}{x}.\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} + \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}.\left( {\frac{{x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - y}}{{{x^2}}}} \right) = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}.\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} + \frac{{x{{\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)}^2} -y \left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)}}{{{x^2}}} =0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow xy\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} + x{\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)^2} - y\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right) = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow xyy'' + x{\left( {y'} \right)^2} - yy' = 0{/tex}

question_text: Solve the differential equation (1 + y 2 ) tan -1 x dx + 2y (1 + x 2 ) dy = 0.
question_html: Solve the differential equation (1 + y2) tan-1x dx + 2y (1 + x2) dy = 0.
solution_text: Given differential equation is (1 + y 2 ) tan -1 x dx + 2y (1 + x 2 ) dy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 + {y^2}} \right){\tan ^{ - 1}}xdx = - 2y\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)dy{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{{\tan }^{ - 1}}xdx}}{{1 + {x^2}}} = - \frac{{2y}}{{1 + {y^2}}}dy{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int {\frac{{{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x}}{{1 + {x^2}}}dx = - \int {\frac{{2y}}{{1 + {y^2}}}dy} } {/tex} Put {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}x = t\;in\,\,LHS,{/tex} we get {tex}\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}dx = dt{/tex} and put 1 + y 2 = u in RHS, we get 2ydy = du {tex} \Rightarrow \int {tdt = - \int {\frac{1}{u} \Rightarrow \frac{{{t^2}}}{2} = - \log u + C} } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}{\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)^2} = - \log \left( {1 + {y^2}} \right) + C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}{\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)^2} + \log \left( {1 + {y^2}} \right) = C{/tex}
solution_html:
Given differential equation is

(1 + y2) tan-1x dx + 2y (1 + x2) dy = 0

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 + {y^2}} \right){\tan ^{ - 1}}xdx = - 2y\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)dy{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{{\tan }^{ - 1}}xdx}}{{1 + {x^2}}} = - \frac{{2y}}{{1 + {y^2}}}dy{/tex}

On integrating both sides, we get

{tex}\int {\frac{{{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x}}{{1 + {x^2}}}dx = - \int {\frac{{2y}}{{1 + {y^2}}}dy} } {/tex}

Put {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}x = t\;in\,\,LHS,{/tex} we get

{tex}\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}dx = dt{/tex}

and put 1 + y2 = u in RHS, we get

2ydy = du

{tex} \Rightarrow \int {tdt = - \int {\frac{1}{u} \Rightarrow \frac{{{t^2}}}{2} = - \log u + C} } {/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}{\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)^2} = - \log \left( {1 + {y^2}} \right) + C{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}{\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)^2} + \log \left( {1 + {y^2}} \right) = C{/tex}

question_text: Find the differential equation of system of concentric circles with centre (1, 2).
question_html: Find the differential equation of system of concentric circles with centre (1, 2).
solution_text: The family of concentric circles with centre (1, 2) and radius a is given by (x - 1) 2 + (y - 2) 2 = a 2 {tex}\Rightarrow {x^2} + 1 - 2x + {y^2} + 4 - 4y = {a^2}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} + {y^2} - 2x - 4y + 5 = {a^2}{/tex} ...(i) On differentiating Eq. (i) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}2x + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - 2 - 4\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {2y - 4} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + 2x - 2 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {y - 2} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \left( {x - 1} \right) = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
The family of concentric circles with centre (1, 2) and radius a is given by

(x - 1)2 + (y - 2)2 = a2

{tex}\Rightarrow {x^2} + 1 - 2x + {y^2} + 4 - 4y = {a^2}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} + {y^2} - 2x - 4y + 5 = {a^2}{/tex} ...(i)

On differentiating Eq. (i) w.r.t. x, we get

{tex}2x + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - 2 - 4\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {2y - 4} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + 2x - 2 = 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {y - 2} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \left( {x - 1} \right) = 0{/tex}

question_text: Solve : {tex}y+\frac{d}{d x}(x y){/tex} = x (sin x + log x)
question_html: Solve : {tex}y+\frac{d}{d x}(x y){/tex} = x (sin x + log x)
solution_text: Given equation is; y + {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (xy) = x(sin x + log x) Now, {tex} \frac{d}{d x}(x y)=y+x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} Putting it back in original given differential equation {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y + y + x {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = x(sin x + log x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} + 2y = x(sin x + log x) Divide by x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2}{x}\right) y{/tex} = sin x + log x Compare {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + {tex}(\frac{2}{x}){/tex} y = sin x + log x Here, P = {tex}\frac{2}{x}{/tex} and Q = sin x + log x This is linear differential equation where P and Q are functions of x For the solution of linear differential equation, we first need to find the integrating factor {tex}\therefore{/tex} IF = {tex}e^{2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} IF = x 2 The solution of linear differential equation is given by y(IF) = {tex}\int Q(IF) d x+C{/tex} Substituting values for Q and IF {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} yx 2 = {tex}\int(\sin x+\log x) x^{2} d x+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} yx 2 = {tex}\int x^{2} \sin x d x+\int x^{2} \log x d x+c{/tex} .....(i) Let us find the integrals {tex}\int x^{2} \sin x d x{/tex} and {tex}\int x^{2} \log x d x{/tex} individually {tex}\therefore \int x^{2} \sin x d x=x^{2}(-\cos x)-\int 2 x(-\cos x) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int x^{2} \sin x d x=-x^{2} \cos x+2 \int x \cos x d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int x^{2} \sin x d x{/tex} = -x 2 cosx + 2(xsinx - {tex}\int \sin x d x{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow \int x^{2} \sin x d x{/tex} = -x 2 cosx + 2(xsinx – (-cosx)) {tex}\Rightarrow \int x^{2} \sin x d x{/tex} = -x 2 cosx + 2xsinx +2cosx …(ii) Now {tex}\int x^{2} \log x d x{/tex} Again, using product rule {tex}\Rightarrow \int x^{2} \log x d x=\log {x}\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right)-\int\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right) d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int x^{2} \log x d x=\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right) \log x-\frac{1}{3} \int x^{2} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int x^{2} \log x d x=\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right) \log x-\frac{x^{3}}{9}{/tex} .....(iii) Substitute (ii) and (iii) in (a) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} yx 2 = -x 2 cos x + 2x sin x + 2 cos x + {tex}\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right) \log x-\frac{x^{3}}{9}+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=-\cos \mathrm{x}+\frac{2 \sin \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}+\frac{2 \cos \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}+\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{3}\right) \log \mathrm{x}-\frac{\mathrm{x}}{9}+\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given equation is;
y + {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex}(xy) = x(sin x + log x)
Now,
{tex} \frac{d}{d x}(x y)=y+x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} 
Putting it back in original given differential equation
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y + y + x{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = x(sin x + log x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} + 2y = x(sin x + log x)
Divide by x
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(\frac{2}{x}\right) y{/tex} = sin x + log x
Compare {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + {tex}(\frac{2}{x}){/tex}y = sin x + log x
Here, P = {tex}\frac{2}{x}{/tex} and Q = sin x + log x
This is linear differential equation where P and Q are functions of x
For the solution of linear differential equation, we first need to find the integrating factor
{tex}\therefore{/tex} IF = {tex}e^{2 \int \frac{1}{x} d x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} IF = x2
The solution of linear differential equation is given by y(IF) = {tex}\int Q(IF) d x+C{/tex} 
Substituting values for Q and IF
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} yx2 = {tex}\int(\sin x+\log x) x^{2} d x+c{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} yx2 = {tex}\int x^{2} \sin x d x+\int x^{2} \log x d x+c{/tex} .....(i)
Let us find the integrals {tex}\int x^{2} \sin x d x{/tex} and {tex}\int x^{2} \log x d x{/tex} individually 
{tex}\therefore \int x^{2} \sin x d x=x^{2}(-\cos x)-\int 2 x(-\cos x) d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \int x^{2} \sin x d x=-x^{2} \cos x+2 \int x \cos x d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \int x^{2} \sin x d x{/tex} = -x2cosx + 2(xsinx - {tex}\int \sin x d x{/tex} )
{tex}\Rightarrow \int x^{2} \sin x d x{/tex} = -x2cosx + 2(xsinx – (-cosx))
{tex}\Rightarrow \int x^{2} \sin x d x{/tex} = -x2cosx + 2xsinx +2cosx …(ii)
Now {tex}\int x^{2} \log x d x{/tex} 
Again, using product rule
{tex}\Rightarrow \int x^{2} \log x d x=\log {x}\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right)-\int\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right) d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \int x^{2} \log x d x=\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right) \log x-\frac{1}{3} \int x^{2} d x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \int x^{2} \log x d x=\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right) \log x-\frac{x^{3}}{9}{/tex} .....(iii)
Substitute (ii) and (iii) in (a)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} yx2 = -x2 cos x + 2x sin x + 2 cos x + {tex}\left(\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right) \log x-\frac{x^{3}}{9}+C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=-\cos \mathrm{x}+\frac{2 \sin \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}+\frac{2 \cos \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}+\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{3}\right) \log \mathrm{x}-\frac{\mathrm{x}}{9}+\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{/tex}

question_text: Find the general solution of (1 + tan y)(dx - dy) + 2x dy = 0
question_html: Find the general solution of (1 + tan y)(dx - dy) + 2x dy = 0
solution_text: Given differential equation is (1 + tan y)(dx - dy) + 2x dy = 0 On dividing throughout by dy, we get {tex}\left( {1 + \tan y} \right)\left( {\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} - 1} \right) + 2x = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left( {1 + \tan y} \right)\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} - \left( {1 + \tan y} \right) + 2x = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left( {1 + \tan y} \right)\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} + 2x = \left( {1 + \tan y} \right){/tex} dividing both sides by (1 + tan y) {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} + \frac{{2x}}{{1 + \tan y}} = 1{/tex} Which is a linear differential equation. On comparing it with {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} + Px = Q{/tex} , we get {tex}P = \frac{2}{{1 + \tan y}},Q = 1{/tex} {tex}I.F = {e^{\int {\frac{2}{{1 + \tan y}}dy} }} = {e^{\int {\frac{{2\cos y}}{{\cos y + \sin y}}dy} }}{/tex} {tex}= {e^{\int {\frac{{\cos y + \sin y + \cos y - \sin y}}{{\cos y + \sin y}}dy} }}{/tex} {tex}= {e^{\int {\left( {1 + \frac{{\cos y - \sin y}}{{\cos y + \sin y}}} \right)dy} }} = {e^{y + \log \left( {\cos y + \sin y} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}= {e^y}.\left( {\cos y + \sin y} \right)\left[ {\because {e^{\log x}} = x} \right]{/tex} The general solution is {tex}x{e^y}\left( {\cos y + \sin y} \right) = \int {1.{e^y}\left( {\cos y + \sin y} \right)dy + C} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x{e^y}\left( {\cos y + \sin y} \right) = \int {{e^y}\left( {\sin y + \cos y} \right)dy + C} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x{e^y}\left( {\cos y + \sin y} \right) = {e^y}\sin y + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \int {{e^x}\left\{ {f\left( x \right) + f'\left( x \right)} \right\}} dx = {e^x}f\left( x \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x\left( {\sin y + \cos y} \right) = \sin y + C{e^{ - y}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given differential equation is (1 + tan y)(dx - dy) + 2x dy = 0

On dividing throughout by dy, we get

{tex}\left( {1 + \tan y} \right)\left( {\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} - 1} \right) + 2x = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \left( {1 + \tan y} \right)\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} - \left( {1 + \tan y} \right) + 2x = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \left( {1 + \tan y} \right)\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} + 2x = \left( {1 + \tan y} \right){/tex}

dividing both sides by (1 + tan y)

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} + \frac{{2x}}{{1 + \tan y}} = 1{/tex}

Which is a linear differential equation.

On comparing it with {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} + Px = Q{/tex}, we get

{tex}P = \frac{2}{{1 + \tan y}},Q = 1{/tex}

{tex}I.F = {e^{\int {\frac{2}{{1 + \tan y}}dy} }} = {e^{\int {\frac{{2\cos y}}{{\cos y + \sin y}}dy} }}{/tex}

{tex}= {e^{\int {\frac{{\cos y + \sin y + \cos y - \sin y}}{{\cos y + \sin y}}dy} }}{/tex}

{tex}= {e^{\int {\left( {1 + \frac{{\cos y - \sin y}}{{\cos y + \sin y}}} \right)dy} }} = {e^{y + \log \left( {\cos y + \sin y} \right)}}{/tex}

{tex}= {e^y}.\left( {\cos y + \sin y} \right)\left[ {\because {e^{\log x}} = x} \right]{/tex}

The general solution is

{tex}x{e^y}\left( {\cos y + \sin y} \right) = \int {1.{e^y}\left( {\cos y + \sin y} \right)dy + C} {/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow x{e^y}\left( {\cos y + \sin y} \right) = \int {{e^y}\left( {\sin y + \cos y} \right)dy + C} {/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow x{e^y}\left( {\cos y + \sin y} \right) = {e^y}\sin y + C{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \int {{e^x}\left\{ {f\left( x \right) + f'\left( x \right)} \right\}} dx = {e^x}f\left( x \right)} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow x\left( {\sin y + \cos y} \right) = \sin y + C{e^{ - y}}{/tex}

question_text: Solve : {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = cos(x + y) + sin (x + y). [Hint: Substitute x + y = z]
question_html: Solve : {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = cos(x + y) + sin (x + y). [Hint: Substitute x + y = z]
solution_text: We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = cos (x + y) + sin (x + y) Using the hint given and substituting x + y = z {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d(z-x)}{d x}{/tex} = cos z + sin z Differentiating z – x with respect to x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d z}{d x}{/tex} - 1 = cos z + sin z {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d z}{d x}{/tex} = 1 + cos z + sin z {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d z}{1+\cos z+\sin z}{/tex} = dx Integrating both sides, we have {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d z}{1+\cos z+\sin z}=\int d x{/tex} We know that cos 2z = 2 cos 2 z – 1 and sin 2z = 2 sin z cos z {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d z}{1+2 \cos ^{2} \frac{z}{2}-1+2 \sin \frac{z}{2} \cos \frac{z}{2}}=x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d z}{2 \cos ^{2} \frac{z}{2}+2 \sin \frac{z}{2} \cos \frac{z}{2}}=x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d z}{2 \cos \frac{z}{2}\left(\cos \frac{z}{2}+\sin \frac{z}{2}\right)}=x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d z}{2 \cos ^{2} \frac{z}{2}\left(1+\frac{\sin \frac{z}{2}}{\cos \frac{z}{2}}\right)}=x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{\sec ^{2} \frac{2}{2} d z}{2\left(1+\tan \frac{z}{2}\right)}=x{/tex} Let 1 + {tex}\tan \frac{z}{2}{/tex} = t Differentiating with respect to z, we get {tex}\frac{d t}{d z}=\frac{\sec ^{2} \frac{z}{2}}{2}{/tex} Hence {tex}\frac{\sec ^{2} \frac{z}{2} d z}{2}=d t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}}=\mathrm{x}{/tex} ⇒ log t + c = x Resubstituting t {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(1+\tan \frac{z}{2}\right)+c=x{/tex} Resubstitute z {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(1+\tan \frac{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}}{2}\right)+\mathrm{c}=\mathrm{x}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = cos (x + y) + sin (x + y)
Using the hint given and substituting x + y = z
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d(z-x)}{d x}{/tex} = cos z + sin z
Differentiating z – x with respect to x
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d z}{d x}{/tex} - 1 = cos z + sin z
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d z}{d x}{/tex} = 1 + cos z + sin z
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d z}{1+\cos z+\sin z}{/tex} = dx
Integrating both sides, we have
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d z}{1+\cos z+\sin z}=\int d x{/tex} 
We know that cos 2z = 2 cos2z – 1 and sin 2z = 2 sin z cos z
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d z}{1+2 \cos ^{2} \frac{z}{2}-1+2 \sin \frac{z}{2} \cos \frac{z}{2}}=x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d z}{2 \cos ^{2} \frac{z}{2}+2 \sin \frac{z}{2} \cos \frac{z}{2}}=x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d z}{2 \cos \frac{z}{2}\left(\cos \frac{z}{2}+\sin \frac{z}{2}\right)}=x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{d z}{2 \cos ^{2} \frac{z}{2}\left(1+\frac{\sin \frac{z}{2}}{\cos \frac{z}{2}}\right)}=x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{\sec ^{2} \frac{2}{2} d z}{2\left(1+\tan \frac{z}{2}\right)}=x{/tex} 
Let 1 + {tex}\tan \frac{z}{2}{/tex} = t 
Differentiating with respect to z, we get
 {tex}\frac{d t}{d z}=\frac{\sec ^{2} \frac{z}{2}}{2}{/tex} 
Hence {tex}\frac{\sec ^{2} \frac{z}{2} d z}{2}=d t{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{\mathrm{dt}}{\mathrm{t}}=\mathrm{x}{/tex} 
⇒ log t + c = x
Resubstituting t
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(1+\tan \frac{z}{2}\right)+c=x{/tex} 
Resubstitute z
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(1+\tan \frac{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}}{2}\right)+\mathrm{c}=\mathrm{x}{/tex}

question_text: Find the general solution of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 3y = sin 2x
question_html: Find the general solution of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 3y = sin 2x
solution_text: Given equation is {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 3y = sin 2x Compare {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 3y = sin 2x with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} we get P = -3 and Q = sin2x This is linear differential equation where P and Q are functions of x Now, IF = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} IF = {tex}e^{\int(-3) d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} IF = e -3x The solution of linear differential equation is given by y(IF) = {tex}\int \mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{IF}) \mathrm{d} x+\mathrm{c}{/tex} ⇒ ye -3x = {tex}\int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x{/tex} ......(i) Let I = {tex}\int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x{/tex} {tex}=\mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}} \int \sin 2 \mathrm{x}-\int[\left(\mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}}\right)^{\prime} \int \sin 2 \mathrm{x}] dx{/tex} = {tex}-\mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}} \frac{\cos 2 \mathrm{x}}{2}+\int 3 \mathrm{e}^{-3 \times \frac{\cos 2 \mathrm{x}}{2}}+\mathrm{c}{/tex} Again, applying the above stated rule in {tex}\int 3 e^{-3 x} \frac{\cos 2 x}{2}{/tex} we get = {tex}-\mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}} \frac{\cos 2 \mathrm{x}}{2}-\frac{3}{2}\left[\mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}} \frac{\sin 2 \mathrm{x}}{2}+\int 3 \mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}} \frac{\sin 2 \mathrm{x}}{2}\right]+\mathrm{c}{/tex} So, {tex}\int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x{/tex} = {tex}-\mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}} \frac{\cos 2 \mathrm{x}}{2}-\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}} \frac{\sin 2 \mathrm{x}}{2}-\frac{9}{4} \int \mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}} \sin 2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{c}{/tex} {tex}\int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x+\frac{9}{4} \int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x{/tex} = {tex}-e^{-3 x} \frac{\cos 2 x}{2}-\frac{3}{2} e^{-3 x} \frac{\sin 2 x}{2}+c{/tex} {tex}\frac{13}{4} \int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x{/tex} = {tex}-e^{-3 x} \frac{\cos 2 x}{2}-\frac{3}{2} e^{-3 x} \frac{\sin 2 x}{2}+c{/tex} {tex}\frac{13}{4} \times 4 \int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x{/tex} = -e -3x 2 cos 2x - 3 e -3x sin 2x + c {tex}13 \int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x{/tex} = -e -3x (2 cos 2x + 3 sin 2x) + c {tex}\int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-\mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}}(2 \cos 2 \mathrm{x}+3 \sin 2 \mathrm{x})}{13}+\mathrm{c}{/tex} Put this value in (i) to get, ye -3x = {tex}\int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{ye}^{-3 \mathrm{x}}=\frac{-\mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}}(2 \cos 2 \mathrm{x}+3 \sin 2 \mathrm{x})}{13}+\mathrm{c}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=-\frac{1}{13}(3 \sin 2 \mathrm{x}+2 \cos 2 \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{ce}^{3 \mathrm{x}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given equation is 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 3y = sin 2x 
Compare {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - 3y = sin 2x with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} 
we get P = -3 and Q = sin2x
This is linear differential equation where P and Q are functions of x
Now, IF = {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} IF = {tex}e^{\int(-3) d x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} IF = e-3x
The solution of linear differential equation is given by y(IF) = {tex}\int \mathrm{Q}(\mathrm{IF}) \mathrm{d} x+\mathrm{c}{/tex} 
⇒ ye-3x = {tex}\int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x{/tex} ......(i)
Let I = {tex}\int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x{/tex} 
{tex}=\mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}} \int \sin 2 \mathrm{x}-\int[\left(\mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}}\right)^{\prime} \int \sin 2 \mathrm{x}] dx{/tex}  
= {tex}-\mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}} \frac{\cos 2 \mathrm{x}}{2}+\int 3 \mathrm{e}^{-3 \times \frac{\cos 2 \mathrm{x}}{2}}+\mathrm{c}{/tex} 
Again, applying the above stated rule in {tex}\int 3 e^{-3 x} \frac{\cos 2 x}{2}{/tex} we get
= {tex}-\mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}} \frac{\cos 2 \mathrm{x}}{2}-\frac{3}{2}\left[\mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}} \frac{\sin 2 \mathrm{x}}{2}+\int 3 \mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}} \frac{\sin 2 \mathrm{x}}{2}\right]+\mathrm{c}{/tex} 
So,
{tex}\int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x{/tex} = {tex}-\mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}} \frac{\cos 2 \mathrm{x}}{2}-\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}} \frac{\sin 2 \mathrm{x}}{2}-\frac{9}{4} \int \mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}} \sin 2 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{c}{/tex} 
{tex}\int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x+\frac{9}{4} \int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x{/tex} = {tex}-e^{-3 x} \frac{\cos 2 x}{2}-\frac{3}{2} e^{-3 x} \frac{\sin 2 x}{2}+c{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{13}{4} \int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x{/tex} = {tex}-e^{-3 x} \frac{\cos 2 x}{2}-\frac{3}{2} e^{-3 x} \frac{\sin 2 x}{2}+c{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{13}{4} \times 4 \int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x{/tex} = -e-3x 2 cos 2x - 3 e-3x sin 2x + c
{tex}13 \int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x{/tex} = -e-3x (2 cos 2x + 3 sin 2x) + c
{tex}\int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-\mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}}(2 \cos 2 \mathrm{x}+3 \sin 2 \mathrm{x})}{13}+\mathrm{c}{/tex}
Put this value in (i) to get,
ye-3x = {tex}\int e^{-3 x} \sin 2 x d x{/tex} 
{tex}\mathrm{ye}^{-3 \mathrm{x}}=\frac{-\mathrm{e}^{-3 \mathrm{x}}(2 \cos 2 \mathrm{x}+3 \sin 2 \mathrm{x})}{13}+\mathrm{c}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=-\frac{1}{13}(3 \sin 2 \mathrm{x}+2 \cos 2 \mathrm{x})+\mathrm{ce}^{3 \mathrm{x}}{/tex}

question_text: Find the equation of a curve passing through (2, 1) if the slope of the tangent to the curve at any point (x, y) is {tex}\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex}
question_html: Find the equation of a curve passing through (2, 1) if the slope of the tangent to the curve at any point (x, y) is {tex}\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex}
solution_text: Slope of tangent is given as; {tex}\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} Slope of tangent of a curve y = f(x) is given by {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} Put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{dx}(vx)=\frac{x^{2}+v^{2} x^{2}}{2 v x^{2}}{/tex} Differentiate vx using product rule, {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\mathrm{v}=\frac{1+\mathrm{v}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{v}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1+\mathrm{v}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{v}}-\mathrm{v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1+\mathrm{v}^{2}-2 \mathrm{v}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{v}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) \frac{1-v^{2}}{2 v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 v}{1-v^{2}} d v=\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) d x{/tex} Integrate {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{2 \mathrm{vdv}}{1-\mathrm{v}^{2}}=\int\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} Put 1 – v 2 = t hence differentiating with respect to v we get {tex}\frac{d t}{d v}{/tex} = -2v which means 2vdv = -dt {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{-d t}{t}{/tex} = log x + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log t = log x + c Resubstitute t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log {tex}\left(1-\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} = log x + c .....(i) Now it is given that the curve is passing through (2, 1) Hence (2, 1) will satisfy the curve equation (i) Putting values x = 2 and y = 1 in (i) {tex}\Rightarrow-\log \left(1-\frac{1^{2}}{2^{2}}\right){/tex} = log 2 + c {tex}\Rightarrow-\log \left(\frac{4-1}{4}\right){/tex} = log 2 + c {tex}\Rightarrow-\log \left(\frac{3}{4}\right){/tex} = log 2 + c {tex}\Rightarrow-\log \left(\frac{3}{4}\right){/tex} - log 2 = c {tex}\Rightarrow-\left(\log \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)+\log 2\right)=c{/tex} Using log a + log b = loga b {tex}\Rightarrow-\log \frac{3}{2}=c{/tex} Put c in equation (i) {tex}\Rightarrow-\log \left(1-\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}\right)=\log \mathrm{x}-\log \frac{3}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\log \left(\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}\right)=\log \mathrm{x}-\log \frac{3}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}\right)^{-1}=\log \mathrm{x}-\log \frac{3}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{x^{2}}{x^{2}-y^{2}}\right)-\log x=-\log \frac{3}{2}{/tex} Using log a – log b = log {tex}\frac{a}{b}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{x}{x^{2}-y^{2}}\right)=-\log \frac{3}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{x}{x^{2}-y^{2}}\right)+\log \frac{3}{2}=0{/tex} Using log a + log b = log ab {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{3 x}{2\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)}\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{3 x}{2\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)}=e^{0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x = 2(x 2 – y 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x = 2x 2 – 2y 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y 2 = 2x 2 – 3x {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{x}^{2}-3 \mathrm{x}}{2}}{/tex} Hence equation of curve is {tex} \mathrm{y}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{x}^{2}-3 \mathrm{x}}{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Slope of tangent is given as; {tex}\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex}
Slope of tangent of a curve y = f(x) is given by {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}+y^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} 
Put y = vx
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{dx}(vx)=\frac{x^{2}+v^{2} x^{2}}{2 v x^{2}}{/tex} 
Differentiate vx using product rule,
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}+\mathrm{v}=\frac{1+\mathrm{v}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{v}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1+\mathrm{v}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{v}}-\mathrm{v}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1+\mathrm{v}^{2}-2 \mathrm{v}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{v}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) \frac{1-v^{2}}{2 v}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 v}{1-v^{2}} d v=\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) d x{/tex} 
Integrate
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{2 \mathrm{vdv}}{1-\mathrm{v}^{2}}=\int\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}{/tex} 
Put 1 – v2 = t hence differentiating with respect to v we get {tex}\frac{d t}{d v}{/tex} = -2v which means 2vdv = -dt
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{-d t}{t}{/tex} = log x + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log t = log x + c
Resubstitute t
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -log {tex}\left(1-\frac{y^{2}}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} = log x + c .....(i)
Now it is given that the curve is passing through (2, 1)
Hence (2, 1) will satisfy the curve equation (i)
Putting values x = 2 and y = 1 in (i)
{tex}\Rightarrow-\log \left(1-\frac{1^{2}}{2^{2}}\right){/tex} = log 2 + c
{tex}\Rightarrow-\log \left(\frac{4-1}{4}\right){/tex} = log 2 + c
{tex}\Rightarrow-\log \left(\frac{3}{4}\right){/tex} = log 2 + c
{tex}\Rightarrow-\log \left(\frac{3}{4}\right){/tex} - log 2 = c
{tex}\Rightarrow-\left(\log \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)+\log 2\right)=c{/tex}
Using log a + log b = loga b
{tex}\Rightarrow-\log \frac{3}{2}=c{/tex} 
Put c in equation (i)
{tex}\Rightarrow-\log \left(1-\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}\right)=\log \mathrm{x}-\log \frac{3}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow-\log \left(\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}\right)=\log \mathrm{x}-\log \frac{3}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}\right)^{-1}=\log \mathrm{x}-\log \frac{3}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{x^{2}}{x^{2}-y^{2}}\right)-\log x=-\log \frac{3}{2}{/tex} 
Using log a – log b = log {tex}\frac{a}{b}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{x}{x^{2}-y^{2}}\right)=-\log \frac{3}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{x}{x^{2}-y^{2}}\right)+\log \frac{3}{2}=0{/tex} 
Using log a + log b = log ab
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left(\frac{3 x}{2\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)}\right)=0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{3 x}{2\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)}=e^{0}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x = 2(x2 – y2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x = 2x2 – 2y2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y2 = 2x2 – 3x
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{x}^{2}-3 \mathrm{x}}{2}}{/tex} 
Hence equation of curve is {tex} \mathrm{y}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{x}^{2}-3 \mathrm{x}}{2}}{/tex}

question_text: Find the equation of the curve through the point (1, 0) if the slope of the tangent to the curve at any point (x, y) is {tex}\frac{{y - 1}}{{{x^2} + x}}{/tex}
question_html: Find the equation of the curve through the point (1, 0) if the slope of the tangent to the curve at any point (x, y) is {tex}\frac{{y - 1}}{{{x^2} + x}}{/tex}
solution_text: It is given that, slope of tangent to the curve at any point (x, y) is {tex}\frac{{y - 1}}{{{x^2} + x}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)_{\left( {x,y} \right)}} = \frac{{y - 1}}{{{x^2} + x}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{y - 1}}{{{x^2} + x}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{y - 1}} = \frac{{dx}}{{{x^2} + x}}{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int {\frac{{dy}}{{y - 1}}} = \int {\frac{{dx}}{{{x^2} + x}}} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \int {\frac{{dy}}{{y - 1}}} = \int {\frac{{dx}}{{x\left( {x + 1} \right)}}} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int {\frac{{dy}}{{y - 1}}} = \int {\left( {\frac{1}{x} - \frac{1}{{x + 1}}} \right)dx} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left( {y - 1} \right) = \log x - \log \left( {x + 1} \right) + \log C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left( {y - 1} \right) = \log \left( {\frac{{xC}}{{x + 1}}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\ y-1= \frac{xC}{x+1}{/tex} Since, the given curve passes through point (1,0) {tex}\therefore 0 - 1 = \frac{{1.C}}{{1 + 1}} \Rightarrow C = - 2{/tex} The particular solution is {tex}y - 1 = \frac{{ - 2x}}{{x + 1}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left( {y - 1} \right)\left( {x + 1} \right) = - 2x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left( {y - 1} \right)\left( {x + 1} \right) + 2x = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that, slope of tangent to the curve at any point (x, y) is {tex}\frac{{y - 1}}{{{x^2} + x}}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore {\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)_{\left( {x,y} \right)}} = \frac{{y - 1}}{{{x^2} + x}}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{y - 1}}{{{x^2} + x}}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{y - 1}} = \frac{{dx}}{{{x^2} + x}}{/tex}

On integrating both sides, we get

{tex}\int {\frac{{dy}}{{y - 1}}} = \int {\frac{{dx}}{{{x^2} + x}}} {/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \int {\frac{{dy}}{{y - 1}}} = \int {\frac{{dx}}{{x\left( {x + 1} \right)}}} {/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \int {\frac{{dy}}{{y - 1}}} = \int {\left( {\frac{1}{x} - \frac{1}{{x + 1}}} \right)dx} {/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left( {y - 1} \right) = \log x - \log \left( {x + 1} \right) + \log C{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left( {y - 1} \right) = \log \left( {\frac{{xC}}{{x + 1}}} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow\ y-1= \frac{xC}{x+1}{/tex}

Since, the given curve passes through point (1,0)

{tex}\therefore 0 - 1 = \frac{{1.C}}{{1 + 1}} \Rightarrow C = - 2{/tex}

The particular solution is {tex}y - 1 = \frac{{ - 2x}}{{x + 1}}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \left( {y - 1} \right)\left( {x + 1} \right) = - 2x{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \left( {y - 1} \right)\left( {x + 1} \right) + 2x = 0{/tex}
