question_text: Find the equation of a curve passing through origin if the slope of the tangent to the curve at any point (x, y) is equal to the square of the difference of the abscissa and ordinate of the point.
question_html: Find the equation of a curve passing through origin if the slope of the tangent to the curve at any point (x, y) is equal to the square of the difference of the abscissa and ordinate of the point.
solution_text: Abscissa means the x-coordinate and ordinate means the y-coordinate Slope of tangent is given as square of the difference of the abscissa and ordinate Difference of abscissa and ordinate is (x - y) and its square will be (x - y) 2 Hence slope of tangent is (x - y) 2 Slope of tangent of a curve y = f(x) is given by {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(x-y)^{2}{/tex} ... (i) Put x - y = z, and differentiating (i) {tex}\Rightarrow 1-\frac{d z}{d x}=z^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - z 2 = {tex}\frac{d z}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{z}}{1-\mathrm{z}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = {tex}\frac{d z}{(1+z)(1-z)}{/tex} .....(i) Using partial fraction for {tex}\frac{1}{(1+z)(1-z)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{(1+z)(1-z)}=\frac{A}{1+z}+\frac{B}{1-z}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{(1+z)(1-z)}=\frac{A(1-z)+B(1+z)}{(1+z)(1+z)}{/tex} Equating numerator {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A(1 - z) + B(1 + z) = 1 Put z = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Put z = -1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Hence {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{(1+z)(1-z)}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{1+z}+\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{1-z}{/tex} Put {tex}\frac{1}{(1+z)(1-z)}{/tex} in (i) {tex}\Rightarrow d x=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{1+z}+\frac{1}{1-z}\right) d z{/tex} Integrate {tex}\Rightarrow \int \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\int \frac{1}{1+\mathrm{z}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{z}+\int \frac{1}{1-\mathrm{z}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{z}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (log(1 + z) + (-log(1 - z)) + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = log(1 + z) - log(1 - z) + c Using log a - log b = log {tex}\frac{a}{b}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{x}=\log \left(\frac{1+\mathrm{z}}{1-\mathrm{z}}\right)+\mathrm{c}{/tex} Resubstitute z = x - y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = log {tex}\left(\frac{1+x-y}{1-(x-y)}\right){/tex} + c.....(ii) Now it is given that the curve is passing through origin that is (0, 0) Hence (0, 0) will satisfy the curve equation (ii) Putting values x = 0 and y = 0 in (ii) {tex}\Rightarrow 2(0)=\log \left(\frac{1+0-0}{1-(0-0)}\right)+c{/tex} ⇒ c = 0 Put c = 0 back in equation (ii) {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{x}=\log \left(\frac{1+\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}}{1-(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y})}\right)+0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{x}=\log \left(\frac{1+\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}}{1-\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}=\frac{1+\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}}{1-\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e 2x (1 - x + y) = (1 + x - y) Hence equation of curve is e 2x (1 - x + y) = (1 + x - y)
solution_html:
Abscissa means the x-coordinate and ordinate means the y-coordinate
Slope of tangent is given as square of the difference of the abscissa and ordinate
Difference of abscissa and ordinate is (x - y) and its square will be (x - y)2
Hence slope of tangent is (x - y)2
Slope of tangent of a curve y = f(x) is given by {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(x-y)^{2}{/tex} ... (i)
Put x - y = z, and differentiating (i)
{tex}\Rightarrow 1-\frac{d z}{d x}=z^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 - z2 = {tex}\frac{d z}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{z}}{1-\mathrm{z}^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = {tex}\frac{d z}{(1+z)(1-z)}{/tex} .....(i)
Using partial fraction for {tex}\frac{1}{(1+z)(1-z)}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{(1+z)(1-z)}=\frac{A}{1+z}+\frac{B}{1-z}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{(1+z)(1-z)}=\frac{A(1-z)+B(1+z)}{(1+z)(1+z)}{/tex} 
Equating numerator
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A(1 - z) + B(1 + z) = 1
Put z = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} B = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Put z = -1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Hence
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{(1+z)(1-z)}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{1+z}+\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{1-z}{/tex} 
Put {tex}\frac{1}{(1+z)(1-z)}{/tex} in (i)
{tex}\Rightarrow d x=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{1+z}+\frac{1}{1-z}\right) d z{/tex} 
Integrate
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\int \frac{1}{1+\mathrm{z}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{z}+\int \frac{1}{1-\mathrm{z}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{z}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(log(1 + z) + (-log(1 - z)) + c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = log(1 + z) - log(1 - z) + c
Using log a - log b = log {tex}\frac{a}{b}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{x}=\log \left(\frac{1+\mathrm{z}}{1-\mathrm{z}}\right)+\mathrm{c}{/tex} 
Resubstitute z = x - y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = log{tex}\left(\frac{1+x-y}{1-(x-y)}\right){/tex} + c.....(ii)
Now it is given that the curve is passing through origin that is (0, 0)
Hence (0, 0) will satisfy the curve equation (ii)
Putting values x = 0 and y = 0 in (ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow 2(0)=\log \left(\frac{1+0-0}{1-(0-0)}\right)+c{/tex} 
⇒ c = 0
Put c = 0 back in equation (ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{x}=\log \left(\frac{1+\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}}{1-(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y})}\right)+0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{x}=\log \left(\frac{1+\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}}{1-\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}}=\frac{1+\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}}{1-\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e2x(1 - x + y) = (1 + x - y)
Hence equation of curve is e2x(1 - x + y) = (1 + x - y)

question_text: Find the equation of a curve passing through the point (1, 1). If the tangent drawn at any point P(x, y) on the curve meets the co-ordinate axes at A and B such that P is the mid-point of AB.
question_html: Find the equation of a curve passing through the point (1, 1). If the tangent drawn at any point P(x, y) on the curve meets the co-ordinate axes at A and B such that P is the mid-point of AB.
solution_text: The below figure obtained by the given information Let the coordinate of the point P is (x, y). It is given that, P is mid-point of AB. So, the coordinates of points A and B are (2x, 0) and (0, 2y) respectively. {tex}\therefore {/tex} Slope of {tex}AB = \frac{{0 - 2y}}{{2x - 0}} = - \frac{y}{x}{/tex} Since, the segment AB is a tangent to the curve at P. {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{y}{x}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{y} = - \frac{{dx}}{x}{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\log y = - \log x + \log c{/tex} {tex}\log y = \log \frac{C}{x}{/tex} ...(i) Since, the given curve passes through (1, 1). {tex}\therefore \log 1 = \log \frac{C}{1}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 0 = \log C{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow C = 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \log y = \log \frac{1}{x}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac{1}{x}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow xy = 1{/tex}
solution_html:
The below figure obtained by the given information

Let the coordinate of the point P is (x, y). It is given that, P is mid-point of AB.
So, the coordinates of points A and B are (2x, 0) and (0, 2y) respectively.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Slope of {tex}AB = \frac{{0 - 2y}}{{2x - 0}} = - \frac{y}{x}{/tex}
Since, the segment AB is a tangent to the curve at P.
{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{y}{x}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{y} = - \frac{{dx}}{x}{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\log y = - \log x + \log c{/tex}
{tex}\log y = \log \frac{C}{x}{/tex} ...(i)
Since, the given curve passes through (1, 1).
{tex}\therefore \log 1 = \log \frac{C}{1}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 0 = \log C{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow C = 1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \log y = \log \frac{1}{x}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac{1}{x}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow xy = 1{/tex}

question_text: Solve: {tex}x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y\left( {\log y - \log x + 1} \right){/tex}
question_html: Solve: {tex}x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y\left( {\log y - \log x + 1} \right){/tex}
solution_text: Given, {tex}x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y\left( {\log y - \log x + 1} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y\left( {\log \frac{y}{x} + 1} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{y}{x}\left( {\log \frac{y}{x} + 1} \right){/tex} ...(i) Which is a homogeneous equation. Put {tex}\frac{y}{x} = v{/tex} or y = vx {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} On substituting these values in Eq. (i), we get {tex}v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v\left( {\log v + 1} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v\left( {\log v + 1 - 1} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v\left( {\log v} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{v\log v}} = \frac{{dx}}{x}{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\int {\frac{{dv}}{{v\log v}}} = \int {\frac{{dx}}{x}} {/tex} On putting {tex}\log v = u{/tex} in LHS integral, we get {tex}\frac{1}{v}.dv = du{/tex} {tex}\int {\frac{{du}}{u} = \int {\frac{{dx}}{x}} } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log u = \log x + \log C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log u = \log Cx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow u = Cx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log v = Cx{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) = Cx{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, {tex}x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y\left( {\log y - \log x + 1} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y\left( {\log \frac{y}{x} + 1} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{y}{x}\left( {\log \frac{y}{x} + 1} \right){/tex}...(i)

Which is a homogeneous equation.

Put {tex}\frac{y}{x} = v{/tex} or y = vx

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex}

On substituting these values in Eq. (i), we get

{tex}v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v\left( {\log v + 1} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v\left( {\log v + 1 - 1} \right){/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v\left( {\log v} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{v\log v}} = \frac{{dx}}{x}{/tex}

On integrating both sides, we get

{tex}\int {\frac{{dv}}{{v\log v}}} = \int {\frac{{dx}}{x}} {/tex}

On putting {tex}\log v = u{/tex} in LHS integral, we get

{tex}\frac{1}{v}.dv = du{/tex}

{tex}\int {\frac{{du}}{u} = \int {\frac{{dx}}{x}} } {/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \log u = \log x + \log C{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \log u = \log Cx{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow u = Cx{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \log v = Cx{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \log \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) = Cx{/tex}

question_text: The degree of the differential equation {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}=x \sin \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} is
question_html: The degree of the differential equation {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}=x \sin \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} is

options.explanation: In general terms for a polynomial the degree is the highest power. Degree of differential equation is defined as the highest integer power of highest order derivative in the equation Here the differential equation is {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}=x \sin \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} Now for degree to exist the given differential equation must be a polynomial in some differentials. Here differentials mean {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} or .... {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}}}{/tex} The given differential equation is not polynomial because of the term {tex}\sin \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} and hence degree of such a differential equation is not defined.

question_text: The degree of the differential equation {tex}\left[1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}\right]^{\frac{3}{2}}=\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} is
question_html: The degree of the differential equation {tex}\left[1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}\right]^{\frac{3}{2}}=\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} is

options.explanation: In general terms for a polynomial the degree is the highest power. The differential equation is {tex}\left(1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}=\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} Square both the sides {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{2}\right)^{3}=\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} Now for degree to exist the given differential equation must be a polynomial in some differentials. Here differentials mean {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} or .... {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}}}{/tex} The given differential equation is polynomial in differentials {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} Degree of differential equation is defined as the highest integer power of highest order derivative in the equation. Here the highest derivative is {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} and there is only one term of highest order derivative in the equation which is {tex}(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}})^2{/tex} whose power is 2 hence degree is 2.

question_html: The order and degree of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}+x^{\frac{1}{5}}=0{/tex}, respectively, are

question_html: The differential equation for y = A cos {tex}\alpha{/tex}x + B sin {tex}\alpha{/tex}x, where A and B are arbitrary constants is

question_html:
Solution of the differential equation
tan y sec2 x dx + tan x sec2 ydy = 0 is :

options.explanation: We have, tan y sec 2 xdx + tan x sec 2 ydy = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} tan y sec 2 xdx = - tan x sec 2 ydy {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\sec ^{2} x}{\tan x} d x=\frac{-\sec ^{2} y}{\tan y} d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{\sec ^{2} x}{\tan x} d x=-\int \frac{\sec ^{2} y}{\tan y} d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log tan x = - log tan y + log k {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log tan x + log tan y = log k {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x tan y = k

book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Family y = Ax + A 3 of curves is represented by the differential equation of degree:
question_html: Family y = Ax + A3 of curves is represented by the differential equation of degree:

options.explanation: y = Ax + A 3 Let us find the differential equation representing it so we have to eliminate the constant A Differentiate with respect to x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=A{/tex} Put back value of A in y {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{x}+\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)^{3}{/tex} Now for the degree to exist the differential equation must be a polynomial in some differentials Here differentials mean {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} or .... {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}}}{/tex} The given differential equation is polynomial in differentials {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} The degree of a differential equation is defined as the highest integer power of highest order derivative in the equation The highest derivative is {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} and highest power to it is 3 Hence degree is 3.

question_text: Integrating factor of {tex}\frac{x d y}{d x}{/tex} – y = x 4 - 3x is :
question_html: Integrating factor of {tex}\frac{x d y}{d x}{/tex} – y = x4 - 3x is :

options.explanation: Given differential equation {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} - y = x 4 - 3x Divide throughout by x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}=x^{3}-3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(-\frac{1}{x}\right) y=x^{3}-3{/tex} Compare {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\left(-\frac{1}{x}\right) y{/tex} = x 3 - 3 with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} we get P = - {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} and Q = x 3 - 3 The IF integrating factor is given by {tex}e^{\int P d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{Pdx}}=\mathrm{e}^{-\int \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{Pdx}}=\mathrm{e}^{-\log x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{Pdx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \mathrm{x}^{-1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{Pdx}}=\mathrm{e}^{\log \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\int \mathrm{Pdx}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} Hence IF integrating factor is {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex}

question_html: Solution of {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} - y = 1, y(0) = 1 is given by

options.explanation: Given that, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-y=1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1 + y {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{1+y}=d x{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get log(1 + y) = x + C ...(i) when x = 0 and y = 1, then log 2 = 0 + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = log 2 The required solution is log (1 + y) = x + log 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\log \left(\frac{1+y}{2}\right){/tex} = x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1+y}{2}=e^{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 + y = 2e x k {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2e x - 1

question_text: The number of solutions of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y+1}{x-1}{/tex} when y(1) = 2 is :
question_html: The number of solutions of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y+1}{x-1}{/tex} when y(1) = 2 is :

options.explanation: We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y+1}{x-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{y+1}=\frac{d x}{x-1}{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get log (y + 1) = log (x - 1) - log C C(y + 1) = (x - 1) {tex}\Rightarrow C=\frac{x-1}{y+1}{/tex} Given when x = 1 and y = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0 So, the require solution is x - 1 = 0 Hence, only one solution exists.

On comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , we get. {tex}\therefore IF=e^{\int p d x}=e^{-\int \frac {x}{1-x^{2}}d x}{/tex} Let, 1 - x 2 = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2xdx = -dt {tex}= e^{\frac12{\int \frac{dt}{t} }} = e^{{\frac12} logt} = \sqrt t =\sqrt{1-x^{2}}{/tex}

question_text: tan -1 x + tan -1 y = c is the general solution of the differential equation:
question_html: tan-1 x + tan-1 y = c is the general solution of the differential equation:

options.explanation: If y = f(x) is solution of a differential equation, then differentiating y = f(x) will give the same differential equation. Let us find the differential equation by differentiating y with respect to x. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan -1 x + tan -1 y = c Differentiating with respect to x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{1+x^{2}}+\frac{1}{1+y^{2}}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\left(1+y^{2}\right) d x+\left(1+x^{2}\right) d y}{\left(1+x^{2}\right)\left(1+y^{2}\right) d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + y 2 )dx + (1 + x 2 )dy = 0

question_html: The differential equation y{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + x = c represents:

options.explanation: Given differential equation is {tex}y \frac{d y}{d x}+x=c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ydy = (c - x)dx On integrating both sides, we get {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{2}=c x-\frac{x^{2}}{2}+d{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 2 + x 2 - 2cx - 2d = 0 Hence, it represents a family of circles whose centres are on the x-axis.

options.explanation: Given, {tex}e^{x} \cos y d x-e^{x} \sin y d y=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos y d x=\sin y d y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\sin y}{\cos y} d y=d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tan y d y=d x{/tex} Now, {tex}\int \tan y d y=\int d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow -\log _{e}|\cos y|=x+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \log _{e}\left(\frac{1}{\cos y}\right)=x+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{x}=\frac{1}{\cos y}+c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos y \cdot e^{x}=c{/tex}

question_html: The solution of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y{/tex} = e-x, y(0) = 0

options.explanation: We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y=e^{-x}{/tex} This is a linear differential equation. On comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} we get P = 1, Q = e -x I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}=e^{\int d x}=e^{x}{/tex} So, the general solution is: {tex}y \cdot e^{x}=\int e^{-x} e^{x} d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot e^{x}=\int d x+C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y.e x = x + C ....(i) Given that when x = 0 and y = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 0 = 0 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0 Eq. (i) becomes y.e x = x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = xe -x

options.explanation: We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1+y^{2}}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{1+y^{2}}=\frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} On integrating both sides, we get tan -1 y = tan -1 x + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan -1 y - tan -1 x = C {tex}\Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y-x}{1+x y}\right)=C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y-x}{1+x y}{/tex} = tan C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x = tan C(1 + xy) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - x = k (1 + xy), where, k = tan C

question_text: The integrating factor of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{1+y}{x}{/tex} is:
question_html: The integrating factor of the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{1+y}{x}{/tex} is:

options.explanation: We have, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+y=\frac{1+y}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x}+\frac{y(1-x)}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}-\left(\frac{1-x}{x}\right) y=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} This is a linear differential equation. On comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , we get {tex}P=\frac{-(1-x)}{x}, Q=\frac{1}{x}{/tex} I.F. = {tex}e^{\int P d x}=e^{-\int \frac{1-x}{x} d x}{/tex} {tex}=e^{x-\log x}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{e^{x}}{x}{/tex}

options.explanation: We have, {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=e^{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x}=\frac{e^{x}}{x}{/tex} This is a linear differential equation. On comparing it with {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q{/tex} , we get P = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} and Q = {tex}\frac{e^{x}}{x}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \text { IF}=e^{\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{(\log x)}=x{/tex} So, the general solution is: {tex}y \cdot x=\int \frac{e^{x}}{x} x d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y {tex}\cdot{/tex} x = {tex}\int e^{x} d x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y {tex}\cdot{/tex} x = e x + k {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{e^{x}}{x}+\frac{k}{x}{/tex}

question_html: Find the differential equation of the family of curves y = Ae2x + B.e-2x.
solution_text: Given y = Ae 2x + B.e -2x . Differentiating on both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2A{e^{2x}} - 2B.{e^{ - 2x}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = 4.A{e^{2x}} + 4B{e^{ - 2x}}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = 4y{/tex} i.e., {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - 4y = 0{/tex}

Thus, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = 4y{/tex} i.e., {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - 4y = 0{/tex}

question_text: Given that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y{e^x}{/tex} and x = 0, y = e. Find the value of y when x = 1.
question_html: Given that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y{e^x}{/tex} and x = 0, y = e. Find the value of y when x = 1.
solution_text: Given {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y{e^x} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int {\frac{{dy}}{y}} = \int {{e^x}dx \Rightarrow \log y = {e^x} + c} {/tex} Substituting x = 0 and y = e we get log e = e 0 + c, i.e., c = 0 (as log e =1) Therefore, log y= e x . Now, substituting x = 1 in the above, we get log y = e {tex} \Rightarrow y = {e^e }{/tex} .
solution_html:
Given {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y{e^x} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int {\frac{{dy}}{y}} = \int {{e^x}dx \Rightarrow \log y = {e^x} + c} {/tex}
Substituting x = 0 and y = e we get log e = e0 + c, i.e., c = 0 (as log e =1)
Therefore, log y= ex.
Now, substituting x = 1 in the above, we get log y = e {tex} \Rightarrow y = {e^e }{/tex}.

question_text: Solve the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x}=x^{2}{/tex}
question_html: Solve the differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{x}=x^{2}{/tex}
solution_text: The equation is of the type {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q, which is a linear differential equation. Now I.F. = {tex}\int \frac {1}{x} dx{/tex} = e logx = x Therefore, solution of the given differential equation is y.x = {tex}\int x x^{2} d x{/tex} i.e. yx = {tex}\frac{x^{4}}{4}+c{/tex} Hence y = {tex}\frac{x^{3}}{4}+\frac{c}{x}{/tex}
solution_html:
The equation is of the type {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q, which is a linear differential equation.
Now I.F. = {tex}\int \frac {1}{x} dx{/tex} = elogx = x
Therefore, solution of the given differential equation is
y.x = {tex}\int x x^{2} d x{/tex} i.e. yx = {tex}\frac{x^{4}}{4}+c{/tex} 
Hence y = {tex}\frac{x^{3}}{4}+\frac{c}{x}{/tex}

question_text: Find the equation of a curve whose tangent at any point on it, different from origin, has slope {tex}y + \frac{y}{x}{/tex} .
question_html: Find the equation of a curve whose tangent at any point on it, different from origin, has slope {tex}y + \frac{y}{x}{/tex}.
solution_text: Given {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y + \frac{y}{x} = y\left( {1 + \frac{1}{x}} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{y}} = \left( {1 + \frac{1}{x}} \right)dx{/tex} Integrating on both sides, we get {tex}\int {\frac{{dy}}{y}} = \int {\left( {1 + \frac{1}{x}} \right)dx} {/tex} log y = x + log x + c {tex} \Rightarrow \log \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) = x + c{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x} = {e^{x + c}} = {e^x}.{e^c} \Rightarrow \frac{y}{x} = k.{e^x}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y = kx.{e^x}.{/tex}
solution_html:
Given {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y + \frac{y}{x} = y\left( {1 + \frac{1}{x}} \right){/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{y}} = \left( {1 + \frac{1}{x}} \right)dx{/tex}

Integrating on both sides, we get

{tex}\int {\frac{{dy}}{y}} = \int {\left( {1 + \frac{1}{x}} \right)dx} {/tex}

log y = x + log x + c {tex} \Rightarrow \log \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) = x + c{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x} = {e^{x + c}} = {e^x}.{e^c} \Rightarrow \frac{y}{x} = k.{e^x}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow y = kx.{e^x}.{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
book: Class 12 - Mathematics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Find the equation of a curve passing through the point (1, 1) if the perpendicular distance of the origin from the normal at any point P(x, y) of the curve is equal to the distance of P from the x-axis.
question_html: Find the equation of a curve passing through the point (1, 1) if the perpendicular distance of the origin from the normal at any point P(x, y) of the curve is equal to the distance of P from the x-axis.
solution_text: Let the equation of normal at P(x, y) be Y - y = {tex}\frac{-d x}{d y}(\mathrm{X}-x){/tex} i.e. Y + {tex}X \frac{d x}{d y}-\left(y+x \frac{d x}{d y}\right)=0{/tex} .....(i) Therefore, the length of perpendicular from origin to (i) is {tex}\frac{y+x \frac{d x}{d y}}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{d x}{d y}\right)^{2}}}{/tex} .....(ii) Also distance between P and x-axis is |y|. Thus, we get {tex}\frac{y+x \frac{d x}{d y}}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{d x}{d y}\right)^{2}}}=|y|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(y+x \frac{d x}{d y}\right)^{2}{/tex} = {tex}y^{2}\left[1+\left(\frac{d x}{d y}\right)^{2}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}\left[\frac{d x}{d y}\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)+2 x y\right]{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=0{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{2 x y}{y^{2}-x^{2}}{/tex} Case I : {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = 0 Integrating both sides, we get x = k, Substituting x = 1, we get k = 1. Therefore, x = 1 is the equation of curve (not possible, so rejected). Case II : {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{2 x y}{y^{2}-x^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y^{2}-x^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} Substituting y = vx, we get {tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2} x^{2}-x^{2}}{2 v x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2}-1}{2 v}-v{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-\left(1+v^{2}\right)}{2 v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 v}{1+v^{2}} d v=\frac{-d x}{x}{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get log (1 + v 2 ) = -log x + log c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log (1 + v 2 ) (x) = log c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + v 2 ) x = c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 + y 2 = cx. Substituting x = 1, y = 1, we get c = 2. Therefore, x 2 + y 2 - 2x = 0 is the required equation.
solution_html:
Let the equation of normal at P(x, y) be Y - y = {tex}\frac{-d x}{d y}(\mathrm{X}-x){/tex} i.e.
Y + {tex}X \frac{d x}{d y}-\left(y+x \frac{d x}{d y}\right)=0{/tex} .....(i)
Therefore, the length of perpendicular from origin to (i) is
{tex}\frac{y+x \frac{d x}{d y}}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{d x}{d y}\right)^{2}}}{/tex} .....(ii)
Also distance between P and x-axis is |y|. Thus, we get
{tex}\frac{y+x \frac{d x}{d y}}{\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{d x}{d y}\right)^{2}}}=|y|{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(y+x \frac{d x}{d y}\right)^{2}{/tex} = {tex}y^{2}\left[1+\left(\frac{d x}{d y}\right)^{2}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}\left[\frac{d x}{d y}\left(x^{2}-y^{2}\right)+2 x y\right]{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=0{/tex} 
or {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{2 x y}{y^{2}-x^{2}}{/tex} 
Case I: {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = 0
Integrating both sides, we get x = k, Substituting x = 1, we get k = 1.
Therefore, x = 1 is the equation of curve (not possible, so rejected).
Case II: {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{2 x y}{y^{2}-x^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y^{2}-x^{2}}{2 x y}{/tex} Substituting y = vx, we get
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2} x^{2}-x^{2}}{2 v x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{v^{2}-1}{2 v}-v{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{-\left(1+v^{2}\right)}{2 v}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2 v}{1+v^{2}} d v=\frac{-d x}{x}{/tex} 
Integrating both sides, we get
log (1 + v2) = -log x + log c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log (1 + v2) (x) = log c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 + v2) x = c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = cx. Substituting x = 1, y = 1, we get c = 2.
Therefore, x2 + y2 - 2x = 0 is the required equation.

question_text: Find the equation of a curve passing through {tex}\left( {1,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex} . If the slope of the tangent to the curve at any point P(x, y) is {tex}\frac{y}{x} - {\cos ^2}\frac{y}{x}.{/tex}
question_html: Find the equation of a curve passing through {tex}\left( {1,\frac{\pi }{4}} \right){/tex}. If the slope of the tangent to the curve at any point P(x, y) is {tex}\frac{y}{x} - {\cos ^2}\frac{y}{x}.{/tex}
solution_text: According to the given condition slope of tangent, {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{y}{x} - {\cos ^2}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} ...(i) This is a homogeneous differential equation. Substituting y = vx, we get, {tex}v+x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} Then, From (i),we have, {tex}v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v - {\cos ^2}v {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = - {\cos ^2}v{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\sec ^2}vdv = - \frac{{dx}}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \int {{{\sec }^2}vdv} = \int { - \frac{{dx}}{x}} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \tan v = - \log x + c{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \tan \frac{y}{x} + \log x = c{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\int {{{\sec }^2}vdv} = \int { - \frac{{dx}}{x}} {/tex} Substituting, {tex}x = 1,y = \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} we get c = 1. Thus, we get {tex}\tan \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) + \log x = 1,{/tex} which is the required equation.
solution_html:
According to the given condition
slope of tangent,

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{y}{x} - {\cos ^2}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} ...(i)

This is a homogeneous differential equation. Substituting y = vx, we get, {tex}v+x\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}
Then, From (i),we have,
{tex}v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v - {\cos ^2}v {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = - {\cos ^2}v{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow {\sec ^2}vdv = - \frac{{dx}}{x}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \int {{{\sec }^2}vdv} = \int { - \frac{{dx}}{x}} {/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \tan v = - \log x + c{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \tan \frac{y}{x} + \log x = c{/tex} ...(ii)

{tex}\int {{{\sec }^2}vdv} = \int { - \frac{{dx}}{x}} {/tex}
Substituting,{tex}x = 1,y = \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex} we get c = 1. Thus, we get
{tex}\tan \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right) + \log x = 1,{/tex} which is the required equation.

question_text: Solve {tex}{x^2}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - xy = 1 + \cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right),x \ne 0{/tex} and {tex}x = 1,y = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}
question_html: Solve {tex}{x^2}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - xy = 1 + \cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right),x \ne 0{/tex} and {tex}x = 1,y = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}
solution_text: Given equation can be written as {tex}{x^2}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - xy = 1 + \cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right),x \ne 0{/tex} {tex}{x^2}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - xy = 2cos^2(\frac{y}{2x}),x \ne 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{x^2}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - xy}}{{2{{\cos }^2}\left( {\frac{y}{{2x}}} \right)}} = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{{\sec }^2}\left( {\frac{y}{{2x}}} \right)}}{2}\left[ {{x^2}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - xy} \right] = 1{/tex} Dividing both sides by {tex}{x^3}{/tex} , we get {tex}\frac{{{{\sec }^2}\left( {\frac{y}{{2x}}} \right)}}{2}\left[ {\frac{{x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - y}}{{{x^2}}}} \right] = \frac{1}{{{x^3}}} x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{{\sec }^2}\left( {\frac{y}{{2x}}} \right)}}{2}\left[ {{x^2}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - xy} \right] = 1{/tex} Integrating both sides, we get {tex}\tan \left( {\frac{y}{{2x}}} \right) = \frac{{ - 1}}{{2{x^2}}} + k{/tex} Substituting {tex}x = 1,y = \frac{\pi }{2},{/tex} we get {tex}k = \frac{3}{2}{/tex} therefore, {tex}\tan \left( {\frac{y}{{2x}}} \right) = \frac{1}{{2{x^2}}} + \frac{3}{2}\tan \left( {\frac{y}{{2x}}} \right) {/tex} {tex}= \frac{{ - 1}}{{2{x^2}}} + \frac{3}{2}{/tex} is the required solution.
solution_html:
Given equation can be written as
{tex}{x^2}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - xy = 1 + \cos \left( {\frac{y}{x}} \right),x \ne 0{/tex}
{tex}{x^2}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - xy = 2cos^2(\frac{y}{2x}),x \ne 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{x^2}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - xy}}{{2{{\cos }^2}\left( {\frac{y}{{2x}}} \right)}} = 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{{\sec }^2}\left( {\frac{y}{{2x}}} \right)}}{2}\left[ {{x^2}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - xy} \right] = 1{/tex}
Dividing both sides by {tex}{x^3}{/tex}, we get
{tex}\frac{{{{\sec }^2}\left( {\frac{y}{{2x}}} \right)}}{2}\left[ {\frac{{x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - y}}{{{x^2}}}} \right] = \frac{1}{{{x^3}}} x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{{{\sec }^2}\left( {\frac{y}{{2x}}} \right)}}{2}\left[ {{x^2}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - xy} \right] = 1{/tex}
Integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\tan \left( {\frac{y}{{2x}}} \right) = \frac{{ - 1}}{{2{x^2}}} + k{/tex}
Substituting {tex}x = 1,y = \frac{\pi }{2},{/tex} we get
{tex}k = \frac{3}{2}{/tex}therefore, {tex}\tan \left( {\frac{y}{{2x}}} \right) = \frac{1}{{2{x^2}}} + \frac{3}{2}\tan \left( {\frac{y}{{2x}}} \right) {/tex} {tex}= \frac{{ - 1}}{{2{x^2}}} + \frac{3}{2}{/tex} is the required solution.

question_text: State the type of the differential equation for the equation. {tex}xdy - ydx = \sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} dx{/tex} and solve it.
question_html: State the type of the differential equation for the equation. {tex}xdy - ydx = \sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} dx{/tex} and solve it.
solution_text: Given equation can be written as {tex}xdy = \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} + y} \right)dx{/tex} i.e., {tex}\frac {dy}{dx} = \frac{{\sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} + y}}{x}{/tex} ....(1) Clearly, RHS of (1) is a homogeneous function of degree zero. Therefore, the given equation is a homogeneous differential equation. Substituting y = vx, we get form (1) {tex}v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\sqrt {{x^2} + {v^2}{x^2}} + vx}}{x}{/tex} {tex}i.e.\,\,v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \sqrt {1 + {v^2}} + v{/tex} {tex}x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \sqrt {1 + {v^2}} \Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{\sqrt {1 + {v^2}} }} = \frac{{dx}}{x}{/tex} ....(2) Integrating both sides of (2), we get {tex}\log \left( {v + \sqrt {1 + {v^2}} } \right) = \log x + \log c {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v + \sqrt {1 + {v^2}} = cx{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x} + \sqrt {1 + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{x^2}}}} = cx {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y+ \sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} = c{x^2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given equation can be written as {tex}xdy = \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} + y} \right)dx{/tex} i.e.,

{tex}\frac {dy}{dx} = \frac{{\sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} + y}}{x}{/tex} ....(1)

Clearly, RHS of (1) is a homogeneous function of degree zero. Therefore, the given equation is a homogeneous differential equation. Substituting y = vx, we get form (1)

{tex}v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\sqrt {{x^2} + {v^2}{x^2}} + vx}}{x}{/tex} {tex}i.e.\,\,v + x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \sqrt {1 + {v^2}} + v{/tex}

{tex}x\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \sqrt {1 + {v^2}} \Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{\sqrt {1 + {v^2}} }} = \frac{{dx}}{x}{/tex} ....(2)

Integrating both sides of (2), we get

{tex}\log \left( {v + \sqrt {1 + {v^2}} } \right) = \log x + \log c {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v + \sqrt {1 + {v^2}} = cx{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{x} + \sqrt {1 + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{x^2}}}} = cx {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y+ \sqrt {{x^2} + {y^2}} = c{x^2}{/tex}

question_html: {tex}\left(\frac{e^{-2 \sqrt{x}}}{\sqrt{x}}-\frac{y}{\sqrt{x}}\right) .\frac{d x}{d y}=1,~~(x \neq 0){/tex} when written in the form {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\mathrm{P} y=\mathrm{Q}{/tex} then P = _________.
solution_text: {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}{/tex} The given equation can be written as {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{e^{-2 \sqrt{x}}}{\sqrt{x}}-\frac{y}{\sqrt{x}}{/tex} i.e, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{y}{\sqrt{x}}=\frac{e^{-2 \sqrt{x}}}{\sqrt{x}}{/tex} This is a differential equation of the type {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + Py = Q Clearly, P = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}{/tex}
